abstract: With the rapid development of smartphones and internet technology, social media has become an indispensable part of the modern lives of teenagers, particularly among high school students. Platforms such as Douyin, TikTok, BiliBili, and YouTube occupy a large portion of their time each day. However, with the widespread use of social media, increasing attention has been given to its potential effects on students' cognitive abilities and academic performance. Existing studies indicate that frequent use of social media may significantly impact students' attention, focus, and working memory, which, in turn, affects their academic performance. Therefore, this study aims to investigate the specific effects of social media usage on attention span, focus, and working memory in students' learning processes.

title: Equilibrium Contrastive Learning for Imbalanced Image Classification_supp1-3670635.pdf
authors: Il Yong Chun
abstract: The data imbalance issue is critical in image classification. Contrastive learning (CL) is a predominant technique in image classification, but they showed limited classification performance when the class distribution in a training dataset is skewed (i.e., an imbalanced dataset). Recently, several supervised CL methods have been proposed particularly to promote an ideal regular simplex geometric configuration in the representation space—characterized by intra-class feature collapse and uniform inter-class mean spacing—especially for imbalanced datasets. In particular, existing prototype-based methods include class representatives, i.e., prototypes, as additional samples to achieve a more balanced treatment of all classes. However, the existing supervised CL methods for imbalanced datasets still suffer from two major limitations. First, they do not consider the alignment between the class means/prototypes and linear classifiers, which could lead to poor generalization. Second, existing prototype-based methods treat class prototypes as only one additional sample per class, making their influence depend on the number of class instances in a batch and causing unbalanced contributions across classes. To address these limitations, we propose Equilibrium Contrastive Learning (ECL), a supervised CL framework designed to promote geometric equilibrium in the representation space, where class features, means, and classifiers are harmoniously balanced under data imbalance. The proposed ECL framework uses two main strategies. First, ECL promotes the representation geometric equilibrium (i.e., a regular simplex geometry characterized by collapsed class samples and uniformly distributed class means), while balancing the contributions of class-average features and class prototypes. Second, ECL establishes a classifier-class center geometric equilibrium by aligning classifier weights and class prototypes in the representation space. We ran experiments with three long-tailed natural image datasets, the CIFAR-10-LT, CIFAR-100-LT and ImageNet-LT benchmark datasets, and the two imbalanced medical image classification datasets, the ISIC 2019 benchmark and our constructed lung cancer chest computed tomography dataset. Results with the five imbalanced datasets show that ECL outperforms existing state-of-the-art supervised CL methods designed for imbalanced/long-tailed classification. Codes are available at this link.

title: DS-TopicMAP: A Text Mining Component for Semantic Topic Identification in Processes of Human-computer Interaction
authors: Suzhen Luo, Yuanqing Wang, Xiaofei Liu
abstract: When providing complex information or decision support, dialogue systems based on GenAI often suffer from low information retrieval efficiency for users due to their lengthy and unclear outputs. To address this challenge, this study proposes and evaluates the DS-TopicMAP prototype. This component is an interactive dynamic text mining tool supporting both Chinese and English, capable of real-time refinement and dynamic topic modeling of GenAI multi-turn dialogue outputs. By integrating hybrid word segmentation, dynamic BERTopic modeling, and multi-dimensional visualization modules, DS-TopicMAP extracts core semantic topics from lengthy dialogues to help users access information more efficiently from GenAI outputs. Empirical analysis based on the Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) and Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Model (PLS-SEM) shows that users' perceived ease of use significantly enhances their perceived usefulness (β = 0.197, p = 0.019) and behavioral intention (β = 0.347, p < 0.001), with perceived usefulness having a particularly significant impact on behavioral intention (β = 0.471, p < 0.001). Furthermore, information management efficiency perception and transparency and Interpretability Perception, as key variables, indirectly promote user acceptance by enhancing perceived usefulness (β = 0.423, p < 0.001) and perceived ease of use (β = 0.435, p < 0.001), respectively. This study theoretically and empirically verifies the effectiveness of DS-TopicMAP in improving GenAI interaction efficiency, transparency, and user experience, providing a practical design paradigm and methodological support for building a user-centric, clearly structured, and trustworthy GenAI dialogue system.

title: View-Adaptive Gaussian Splatting with Dynamic Spherical Harmonics Modulation for Multi-Scale Urban Scene Rendering
authors: Jiye Kim, Chanheum Yeon, Haeweon Jung, Eungyeom Yang, Jinho Lee
abstract: Despite recent advances in large-scale novel view synthesis centered around CityGaussian, performance degradation still occurs under extreme view-scale changes such as transitions between aerial and ground-level viewpoints. The core cause of this limitation lies in the use of fixed spherical harmonics (SH) coefficients. When training is dominated by aerial data, SH coefficients tend to converge toward low-frequency representations, resulting in a significant loss of high-frequency details that are essential for near-field views. To address this issue, we propose View-Adaptive Gaussians, a framework compatible with CityGaussian that enables dynamic modulation of SH coefficients conditioned on the viewpoint. Our method consists of two key components. First, we design a lightweight model that takes the camera–Gaussian distance and the camera viewing angle as conditions and dynamically predicts per-band SH weights. This model acts as a view-dependent filter, suppressing aliasing for distant views while encouraging high-frequency details to emerge in close-up views. Second, to ensure that higher-order SH components remain learnable even under strong far-view observation bias, we introduce an adaptive loss term that explicitly preserves SH components. Built on top of the CityGaussian primitive structure, our approach mitigates the scale-conflict problem between aerial and street-level viewpoints and achieves multi-scale consistency while maintaining real-time rendering performance. Experiments on an integrated dataset show that our method improves PSNR by +1.77dB over CityGaussian v2, and also substantially outperforms it in qualitative evaluation. By preserving rendering efficiency while eliminating visual artifacts, our method provides a foundation for deployment in next-generation city-scale simulation.

title: Mechanical and Optical Characterization of the POLARBEAR2b Cryogenic Half-Wave Plate
authors: B. Bixler, K. Arnold, C.A. Hill, A. Kusaka
abstract: The Simons Array was a set of two polarization sensitive millimeter wave astrophysical telescopes located near the peak of Cerro Toco in the Chilean Atacama desert at an elevation of 5300 m. Each telescope employs an off-axis Gregorian-Dragone design, coupled to three reimaging lenses, to focus light from the Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) onto a focal plane populated with 7588 Transition Edge Sensor polarimeters (TES). Near each receiver's vacuum window is a continuously rotating cryogenic half-wave plate (CHWP), a polarization modulator, which reduces the effects of atmospheric noise and allows a single TES to measure both polarization states, eliminating systematics arising from pair-differencing orthogonal detectors. We present optical and mechanical functionality for the CHWP in use in the POLARBEAR2b (PB2b) receiver, one of the two receivers in the array. In particular, we highlight the angle reconstruction algorithm, rotation stability, induced rotation synchronous signal effects, and overall encoded noise levels.

title: Embedded Grating Sensing and Compensation Enabling Cross-Scale Nanopositioning
authors: Siyuan Meng, Jiankang Jiang, Fubo Wang, Gang Wang, Dongmei Wu, Wei Dong, Changhai Ru
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Grating displacement sensing is regarded as one of the key technologies for achieving cross-scale nanopositioning. This paper proposes a real-time grating sensing correction and compensation technology to enhance the performance of the embedded grating displacement sensor in Cross-scale piezoelectric actuators (CSPAs), thereby enabling nano-scale motion positioning of CSPAs. Firstly, based on the principle of diffracted image reflection, a miniaturized grating sensing unit that can be monolithically integrated with the CSPA structure is designed. Secondly, an online self-correction algorithm based on amplitude iteration is proposed to dynamically eliminate DC offset and amplitude imbalance errors in the signals. Furthermore, a real-time error compensation strategy is constructed to compensate for inherent periodic errors and measurement lag errors of the system induced by stick-slip effects. Experimental results demonstrate that, with the proposed technology, the embedded grating displacement sensor can achieve a detection resolution of 0.9 nm within the full stroke. The CSPA integrated with this sensor achieved a positioning accuracy within ±1.3 nm over its scanning range, and a full-stroke bidirectional positioning consistency of 3.093 ± 1.358 nm.

title: Thoth: Uncovering Data-Dependent Memory Access Patterns via Annotation-Directed Load Sampling
authors: Kanheng Jiang, Yongxin Lyu, Zhiyuan Zhang, Zengshi Wang, Chao Fu, Jun Han
publish: ACM Transactions on Architecture and Code Optimization
abstract: None

title: Continuous representation of tactile information by an unsupervised liquid state machine
authors: Katsunori Kitano, Mitsuhito Ando, Haruo Noma
abstract: Tactile information is indispensable for real-world systems such as robots used for manipulating physical objects. Tactile sensation is acquired as a time-series information by mechano-transduction systems, and the system responsible for processing such inputs must effectively process the temporal structure in these acquired signals. However, the implementation of tactile perception remains challenging. Furthermore, because supervised learning is unrealistic for real-world sensory perception systems, understanding the role of unsupervised learning in perception is crucial. In this study, we proposed a liquid state machine with unsupervised learning for tactile perception and examined the mechanism by which it discriminated signals from different materials. The model encoded tactile information into the spatio-temporal dynamics of readout neurons based on texture smoothness. Most sensory perception systems use supervised learning; consequently, their response to unknown inputs remains unclear. The proposed system can predict which known material resembles an unknown input. This feature is essential as real-world systems encounter unfamiliar information and environments.

title: Long-Term Household Electricity Consumption Forecasting via Ensemble Learning Approach: Application of the TreeBagger Model
authors: F. Issi
abstract: Accurate long-horizon electricity consumption forecasting is essential for effective energy management, operational planning, and grid stability. This study proposes a TreeBagger-based ensemble learning framework for monthly residential electricity consumption prediction using an 88-month dataset. The proposed methodology incorporates seasonal memory through lagged consumption variables, cyclic month encoding, and trend components to capture both short-term autoregressive patterns and annual seasonality. To ensure a rigorous evaluation, the proposed model was compared with several baseline forecasting approaches, including ARIMA, Support Vector Regression (SVR), and XGBoost, under a fixed-origin training–testing framework. Experimental results show that the TreeBagger model achieves the best overall predictive performance, producing the lowest RMSE (30.582) and MAPE (15.96%) among the evaluated models, while maintaining competitive MAE values. The findings demonstrate that bagging-based ensemble models are highly effective for modeling the nonlinear and seasonal characteristics of household electricity consumption data. The proposed approach provides a reliable and computationally efficient framework for medium-scale monthly electricity demand forecasting and can support practical applications such as energy planning and consumption analysis.

title: Hybrid Coordination Framework for Centralized Task Allocation and Execution in Heterogeneous Multi-Robot Systems
authors: T. Glace Varghese, Sreeja Kochuvila, Navin Kumar, R. R. Venkatesha Prasad
abstract: Multi-robot coordination has emerged as a critical enabler for efficient warehouse logistics, yet existing approaches struggle to balance solution quality, computational efficiency, and real-time adaptability in heterogeneous robot environments. This paper presents a novel centralized coordination architecture that integrates a hybrid optimization algorithm combining the shapley value clustering algorithm (SVCA) with the non-dominated sorting genetic algorithm II (NSGA-II) for multi-robot task allocation (MRTA). The proposed system addresses the complex challenge of allocating logistics tasks to heterogeneous mobile robots with varying payload capacities, energy consumption rates, and task deadline while simultaneously optimizing multiple conflicting objectives including makespan, energy consumption, deadline adherence, and workload balance. Unlike traditional static scheduling methods, the proposed system includes dynamic task reallocation capabilities that enable robots to autonomously detect and execute pending tasks nearby upon completion, be it with intelligent battery management that triggers autonomous recharging when energy thresholds are reached. Implemented using robot operating system 2 (ROS2) Humble and validated in Gazebo simulation environments with warehouse scenarios involve five heterogeneous robots and multiple logistics tasks. Experimental results show that, with the hybrid algorithm, 100% task allocation can be achieved with an average allocation time of 0.122 seconds, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art methods including island model genetic algorithm (IMGA), SVCA, particle swarm optimization (PSO), genetic algorithm (GA), and standalone NSGA-II executions. The system effectively manages complex logistics operations to automate thewarehouse within the simulated environment, while maintaining required constraint.

title: Active Versus Passive: CRB Constrained Beamforming Design and Reflection Manipulation for RIS Assisted ISAC System
authors: Zhongyu Ma, Xinji Hu, Zhaobin Li, Guangjie Han, Yingting Liu
abstract: This letter investigates the joint design of beamforming and reflection manipulation for the active reconfigurable intelligent surface-assisted integrated sensing and communication (ARIS-ISAC) system, where multi-user communication and sensing tasks are supported by an active RIS and a base station (BS). The sum rate maximization problem is formulated as a complex non-convex optimization problem, subject to constraints on the Cramér-Rao bound (CRB) for sensing accuracy, BS transmit power, and active RIS reflection coefficients. A hybrid approach combining fractional programming (FP), majorization-minimization (MM) and block coordinate descent (BCD) is proposed to solve the decoupled sub-problems iteratively. Simulation results demonstrate that ARIS-ISAC achieves more efficient collaboration between high-precision sensing and reliable communication than its passive counterparts.

title: IEEE Women in Engineering
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Terahertz Science and Technology

title: Josephson Junction Radiators as Spectral Calibrators of Millimeter-Wave Low-Temperature Detectors
authors: Johannes Hubmayr, Shannon M. Duff, Robert McDermott, Corwin Shiu, Gabriel Spahn, David Harrison, Joel N. Ullom, Anna Vaskuri
abstract: The Josephson junction radiator (JJR) is a thin-film superconducting device that converts DC voltage into coherent millimeter-wave (mm-wave) radiation. The source is frequency-tunable, instantaneously narrow-band, compact, lightweight, has no moving parts, and may share the same cryogenic and vacuum space as the device under test. With these favorable characteristics, the JJR may find use as a general purpose calibration tool for low temperature mm-wave detectors. We show that the JJR unambiguously emits mm-wave radiation and couples to a mm-wave detector. We use the JJR to determine the spectral response of a superconducting mm-wave detector that operates in two large frequency passbands centered at 150 GHz and 220 GHz. A comparison to measurements with a conventional Fourier Transform Spectrometer is presented, and the use of the JJR as a precision spectral calibration tool is discussed.

title: Reputation-Based Hyperledger Fabric for Private and Scalable Data Sharing in CAVs
authors: Rahma Hammedi, Devki Nandan Jha, David J Brown, Mohammad Aljaidi, Yue Cao, Omprakash Kaiwartya
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Vehicular Technology
abstract: The operation of Connected and Autonomous Vehicles (CAVs) is primarily driven by the continuous exchange of data from various sources, including in-vehicle sensors, neighbouring vehicles, and roadside infrastructure. This continuous data exchange results in the accumulation of large volumes of data with dimensionality, which is essential for accurate decision-making in autonomous driving functions. Shared data often encompasses highly sensitive data such as precise vehicular location, driver identification and behavioural patterns. As a result, there is an increasing public concern over the privacy implications associated with the extensive data exchanges. In this context, this research investigates access control mechanisms that ensure privacy and trust in CAVs. We propose a Reputation-based Hyperledger Fabric (RepHLF) framework, a novel privacy-preserving architecture that integrates across-channel Hyperledger Fabric blockchain with a dynamic, multi-metric reputation model for CAV environments. The reputation mechanism evaluates vehicles based on three parameters: behavioural integrity, legitimacy, and historical communication reliability. Each parameter evolves using exponential decay functions to reflect temporal relevance. Data access is subsequently restricted to trusted vehicles only, according to their computed reputation index. The performance of RepHLF is evaluated in terms of accuracy, latency, memory usage, privacy loss, and communication overhead using Hyperledger Caliper. Simulation results demonstrate ultra-low average latency of approximately 201 ms, minimal communication cost of 2.8 KB per transaction, Throughput of 4.99 TPS and high learning accuracy of 99.94%, while maintaining a bounded privacy loss of 3.12%. The integrated reputation mechanism within the consensus algorithm further enhances network reliability by dynamically identifying untrusted vehicles and restricting their transaction requests. Further analysis of the proposed model, RepHLF, against existing reputation-based models demonstrates superior dynamic trust evolution and robust privacy preservation.

title: Exploring Student Perceptions on Integrating Multi-Modality in GenAI
authors: Shile Zhang, Shuhan Zhang, Weijian Liang
abstract: The integration of Generative AI (GenAI) into education has sparked interest in its potential to enhance learning efficiency. However, debates persist regarding its effects on students' learning experience and satisfaction. Additionally, challenges, risks, and the role of multi-modality in improving user experience remain underexplored. To address these gaps, we conducted a survey of 202 students (aged 14–23) from two vocational schools and three universities in southern China. The analysis of the survey results validated the User Interaction with Chatbot Interfaces (UICI) questionnaire as a reliable tool and demonstrated that multi-modality—through visual or audio content—can help mitigate challenges in specific scenarios, thereby enhancing student experiences. Five user categories emerged from the findings: Adaptation Optimization (AO), Risk Mitigation (RM), Interest Stimulation (IS), Scenario Support (SS), and Feature Expansion (FE). These insights provide actionable guidance for designing more effective and inclusive educational GenAI tools.

title: NeRF Reconstruction Quality Under Various Image Artifacts
authors: Alexander Xiang
abstract: Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) is a method used to generate novel views of complex 3D scenes by using a neural network to train a continuous 3D representation from a series of 2D images taken from different viewpoints. One of the key concepts to render these scenes involves ray integration, as the NeRF algorithm assumes that the color of each pixel in an image results from the linear integration of reflected light rays from all directions, with the intensity being directly proportional to the amount of light received. For each pixel in each input photo provided to the NeRF, the neural network estimates the camera’s position and casts a ray through the pixel along its path. At each sample points along, the network predicts the color and volume density. These values are then linearly integrated along the ray to determine the final color represented in the reconstructed scene. However, this could be a problem for real world CMOS/CCD urban camera sensors, where illumination can be potentially very bright or saturated. Even without saturation, many sensors utilize non-linear gamma correction to measure sensor response, which might be problematic for the linear integration. In working with the WRIVA program at JHU APL, we want to ask an important question: Does the non-linear response from gamma correction break the physics of NeRF so the traditional integration method won’t work anymore?

title: CIRSense: Rethinking WiFi Sensing With Channel Impulse Response_supp1-3678326.pdf
authors: He Chen
abstract: WiFi sensing based on channel state information (CSI) collected from commodity WiFi devices has shown great potential across a wide range of applications, including vital sign monitoring and indoor localization. Existing WiFi sensing approaches typically estimate motion information directly from CSI. However, they often overlook the inherent advantages of channel impulse response (CIR), a delay-domain representation that enables more intuitive and principled motion sensing by naturally concentrating motion energy and separating multipath components. Motivated by this, we revisit WiFi sensing and introduce CIRSense, a new framework that enhances the performance and interpretability of WiFi sensing with CIR. CIRSense is built upon a new motion model that characterizes fractional delay effects, a fundamental challenge in CIR-based sensing. This theoretical model underpins technical advances for the three challenges in WiFi sensing: hardware distortion compensation, high-resolution distance estimation, and subcarrier aggregation for extended range sensing. CIRSense, operating with a 160 MHz channel bandwidth, demonstrates versatile sensing capabilities through its dual-mode design, achieving a mean error of approximately 0.25 bpm in respiration monitoring and 0.09 m in distance estimation. Comprehensive evaluations across residential spaces, far-range scenarios, and multi-target settings demonstrate CIRSense's superior performance over state-of-the-art CSI-based baselines. Notably, at a challenging sensing distance of 20 m, CIRSense achieves at least <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$3\times$</tex-math></inline-formula> higher average accuracy with more than <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$4.5\times$</tex-math></inline-formula> higher computational efficiency.

title: Disentangled Graph Prompting for Out-Of-Distribution Detection_supp1-3678022.pdf
authors: Cheng Yang
abstract: When testing data and training data come from different distributions, deep neural networks (DNNs) will face significant safety risks in practical applications. Therefore, out-of-distribution (OOD) detection techniques, which can identify OOD samples at test time and alert the system, are urgently needed. Existing graph OOD detection methods usually characterize fine-grained in-distribution (ID) patterns from multiple perspectives, and train end-to-end graph neural networks (GNNs) for prediction. However, due to the unavailability of OOD data during training, the absence of explicit supervision signals could lead to sub-optimal performance of end-to-end encoders. To address this issue, we follow the pre-training+prompting paradigm to utilize pre-trained GNN encoders, and propose Disentangled Graph Prompting (DGP), to capture fine-grained ID patterns with the help of ID graph labels. Specifically, we design two prompt generators that respectively generate class-specific and class-agnostic prompt graphs by modifying the edge weights of an input graph. We also design several effective losses to train the prompt generators and prevent trivial solutions. We conduct extensive experiments on ten datasets to demonstrate the superiority of our proposed DGP, which achieves a relative AUC improvement of 3.63% over the best graph OOD detection baseline. Ablation studies and hyper-parameter experiments further show the effectiveness of DGP.

title: Clover: Workload Verification for Real-Time Detection of Contention-Induced Slowdowns in Serverless Platforms
authors: Junxian Shen, Han Zhang, Weiwei Lin, Yantao Geng, Jiawei Li, Jilong Wang, Mingwei Xu
abstract: Serverless computing, or Function-as-a-Service, continues to gain popularity due to its pay-as-you-go billing model, flexibility, and cost efficiency. However, these same features introduce significant security risks, such as the Denial-of-Wallet (DoW) attack. In this paper, we conduct real-world DoW attacks on commercial serverless platforms to evaluate their severity. To detect such attacks, we design, implement, and evaluate Clover, an accurate and user-friendly DoW detection system with negligible performance overhead. Clover addresses information ambiguity in serverless environments by deploying a request-oriented metric collection agent. At its core, Clover proposes a workload verification approach to bridge performance metrics and execution duration. Specifically, Clover uses a multivariate linear model to learn the benign relationship between metrics and execution duration, effectively characterizing normal workload behavior. It then continuously monitors runtime workloads by calculating their Mahalanobis distance from this learned benign model. Deviations identified through this distance indicate potential DoW attacks. Implemented as a practical system, Clover introduces performance overhead of less than 3.2%, maintains an average model execution time of only 0.84 microseconds, and achieves an accuracy of 92.7% under the most challenging scenario.

title: Research on the Improved Excitation Topology of Pulsed Alternator With Electric Field Energy Storage Device
authors: Yingjie Chen, Youlong Wang, Qianwen Wang, Chen Chen, Qi Li
abstract: In order to ensure the output performance of the pulsed alternator, it is usually necessary to drive it to a higher speed before discharging. However, due to its large moment of inertia, the speed drops less during a single discharge, resulting in limited energy release per discharge. In addition, after the pulsed alternator enters the discharge stage, the excitation current is no longer controlled, and the later stage of discharge shows a trend of attenuation, resulting in a rapid decline in the discharge current waveform. To solve these problems, this article proposes an excitation topology with an electric field energy storage device (EFESD). By connecting the EFESD, which is composed of power semiconductor devices and storage capacitors in parallel to both ends of the field winding of the pulsed alternator, the storage capacitors in the energy storage device are charged and store energy in parallel during the self-excitation process of the pulsed alternator. In the later stage of the pulsed alternator discharging to the load, the storage capacitors are connected in series to discharge to the field winding, so as to realize the compensation of the excitation current, expand the flexibility of the excitation current regulation, and then achieve the adjustment of the output current in the later stage of the pulsed alternator discharging. On this basis, a comparative analysis is carried out for the different working modes of the proposed excitation topology, and the genetic algorithm is used to optimize the relevant parameters, further improving the performance of the system.

title: Beyond Redundancy: Toward Agile Resilience in Optical Networks to Overcome Unpredictable Disasters
authors: Toru Mano, Hideki Nishizawa, Takeo Sasai, Soichiroh Usui, Dmitrii Briantcev, Devika Dass, Brandt Bashaw, Eoin Kenny, Marco Ruffini, Yoshiaki Sone, Koichi Takasugi, Daniel Kilper
abstract: Resilience in optical networks has traditionally relied on redundancy and pre-planned recovery strategies, both of which assume a certain level of disaster predictability. However, recent environmental changes such as climate shifts, the evolution of communication services, and rising geopolitical risks have increased the unpredictability of disasters, reducing the effectiveness of conventional resilience approaches. To address this unpredictability, this article introduces the concept of agile resilience, which emphasizes dynamic adaptability across multiple operators and layers. We identify key requirements and challenges, and present enabling technologies for the realization of agile resilience. Using a field-deployed transmission system, we demonstrate rapid system characterization, optical path provisioning, and database migration within six hours. These results validate the effectiveness of the proposed enabling technologies and confirm the feasibility of agile resilience.

title: Comparative Analysis of Dynamic Models and Adaptive Process-Noise Modelling for Kalman-Based Aerial Target Tracking
authors: Arthur Medeiros Macedo, Karl Heinz Kienitz
abstract: Accurate target tracking is essential for the effectiveness of air surveillance, air-traffic monitoring, and defense-support systems. This paper presents a comparative study of several dynamic motion models applied to Kalman-based filters for aerial target tracking, including constant-velocity, constant-acceleration, correlated-acceleration (Singer), constant-turn, and curvilinear models, as well as a multiple-model estimator based on the Interacting Multiple Model (IMM) framework. An online process-noise covariance matrix adaptation scheme is also proposed, based on a covariance-basis decomposition that enables interpretable adjustment of dynamic uncertainties. The method dynamically updates the process noise covariance matrix to maintain statistical consistency under varying motion regimes. A systematic evaluation is performed using synthetic trajectories and real tracking data. Results show that filters based on models consistent with the underlying dynamics achieve the lowest position errors and best statistical consistency. Moreover, simplified models combined with adaptive process-noise covariance matrix tuning exhibit robust and competitive performance across different motion conditions, while the IMM estimator provides strong performance in scenarios with changing maneuver regimes. The findings highlight the effectiveness of covariance-based adaptation for enhancing Kalman filter robustness in air target tracking applications.

title: A Practical Framework for Control Structure Synthesis and Automated Design Based on Linear Quadratic Tracker: Theory and Application in Power Electronic Systems
authors: Masoud Karimi-Ghartemani, Masoud Davari, S. Ali Khajehoddin, Houshang Karimi
abstract: Classic control system approaches based on the Laplace transform and transfer functions lack a straightforward and convenient process; the designer must simultaneously observe and tradeoff multiple, interwoven criteria, including stability, stability margins, transient response indices, and steady-state errors. Nonetheless, the approaches based on state-space models and state feedback lack a systematic procedure for selecting closed-loop poles or weight matrices when an optimal control approach is employed. This article presents a control structure and a design procedure to address these issues. First, the proposed structure ensures the complete removal of all steady-state errors to a predefined set of commands and disturbances. Afterward, the proposed design procedure determines the entire feedback gains in an automated and convenient way without incurring instability. The overall proposed control system features a systematic design and robust performance, with controlled transient responses. Simulations and experimental results from testing based on power hardware-in-the-loop simulations of various power electronic systems, including inverter-based resources, AC grid-connected converters, and DC/DC converters, along with user-friendly design algorithms and models, are presented to demonstrate the proposed method.

title: Mismatched Pairs Dynamic Correction for Cross-Modal Alignment in Video Moment Retrieval
authors: Hongxiao Hu, Ran Ran, Jiwei Wei, Chengwei Sun, Yuyang Zhou, Shiyuan He, Kuien Liu, Yang Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Video Moment Retrieval (VMR) aims to locate and identify specific moments in a video based on a natural language query. However, most existing methods overlook the issue of mismatched pairs (i.e., false negatives), wherein semantically similar or nearly identical queries and videos are erroneously treated as negative instances. To address this problem, we propose Mismatched Pairs Dynamic Correction (MPDC), a novel approach that rectifies such mismatched pairs on-the-fly without requiring additional annotations or intricate procedures. Specifically, we first construct both intra- and inter-modal similarity matrices for text-text, video-video, and text-video pairs, then aggregate these matrices to effectively detect previously overlooked mismatched pairs. We subsequently address these mismatches by adaptively updating their matching relationships during training, ensuring a dynamic and accurate correction process. Furthermore, to enhance the distinguishability of multi-modal features, we incorporate auxiliary queries derived from these similarity matrices to generate discriminative saliency scores, which serve as weights to refine the original feature representations. Our method is plug-and-play, seamlessly integrating into existing architectures and significantly boosting their performance. Extensive experiments conducted on four benchmark datasets (QVHighlights, Charades-STA, ActivityNet-Captions, and TACoS) demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance.

title: Contrastive Learning for Modeling Sensitive Attributes in Fairness-Aware Recommendation_supp1-3676999.pdf
authors: Shenghao Liu
abstract: Recently, the research on fairness in recommendation systems has garnered widespread attention. Moreover, numerous fair recommendation models have been developed for scenarios with limited sensitive information, thereby alleviating the issue of missing sensitive information. However, the performance of these methods still tends to decline significantly when sensitive attributes are extremely scarce. In this paper, we propose FairCL, a novel fair recommendation framework designed to perform effectively under limited sensitive attribute information. FairCL features a contrastive learning-based sensitive attribute encoder that can be integrated with existing fair recommendation algorithms. By leveraging both collaborative information and item side information, we predict unknown sensitive attributes and apply contrastive learning for sensitive attribute modeling. Furthermore, we theoretically demonstrate how FairCL can be integrated with mutual information-based and adversarial learning-based fairness algorithms. Extensive experiments on three real-world datasets show that FairCL significantly enhances fairness, even when only a small portion of users' sensitive attributes are known. The code and data are at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/CL-for-FairRec-000A/.

title: Physical Layer Security of Coupled Phase Shifts STAR-RIS-Aided NOMA System under Hybrid Far- and Near-Field Scenarios
authors: Yuang Chen, Lei Shi, Zhiqing Tang, Lingfeng Shen, Wanming Hao, Jie Li
abstract: Near-field (NF) communications have attracted considerable interest, particularly with the implementation of extremely large-scale antenna arrays (ELAA). Additionally, the increase in communication frequencies and the expansion of reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) apertures contribute to this growing field. This paper investigates the synergy of simultaneously transmitting and reflecting (STAR)-RIS and non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) for secure transmission under hybrid far-field (FF) and NF scenarios. The secrecy sum rate (SSR) maximization problem is formulated by joint optimization of the power allocation, the beamforming at access point (AP), and the transmission/reflection coefficients (TRCs). Specifically, we consider the transmit power budget, unit-norm conditions, coupled phase shifts (CPS), quality of service requirements, and decoding order. To tackle this extremely challenging problem, we combine the successive convex approximation (SCA), Riemannian exact penalty method via smoothing, and penalty dual decomposition (PDD) and successfully develop an efficient iterative algorithm. Simulation results reveal that the proposed design exhibits superior effectiveness when compared to other traditional benchmarks.

title: Terminology Fragmentation of Infrastructure Calibration in Radio-Based Indoor Positioning: A Systematic Review_supp1-3680224.pdf
authors: Paul Schwarzbach

title: Learning-Based Robust Control of Electro-Ribbon Artificial Muscle
authors: Yuejun Xu, Majid Taghavi, Etienne Burdet
abstract: This article presents a learning-based robust control approach for electro-ribbon artificial muscle systems. The complex and coupled nonlinear electromechanical dynamics of these actuators make accurate modeling difficult. To address this challenge, we employ a neural network to approximate the actuator’s internal dynamics, with weight parameters set during an initial learning phase via gradient descent of a Lyapunov function. Feedback linearization combined with slidingmode control is then used to compensate for model estimation errors and achieve precise trajectory tracking, while an integral action mitigates steadystate uncertainties. A Lyapunov-function-based analysis establishes bounded estimation errors during the learning phase, and uniform ultimate boundedness of the closed-loop tracking errors. Experimental results under various operating conditions validate the novel control scheme, exhibiting improved tracking performance and robustness compared to conventional PID control.

title: Spherical Stacked Intelligent Metasurfaces: A Paradigm for Full-Space Wave-Domain Processing
authors: Jiaqi Zhang, Qingchao Li, Kun Fang, Zhipeng Wang, Deyou Zhang, Hongyu Li, Bruno Clerckx
abstract: This article introduces the concept of spherical stacked intelligent metasurface (S-SIM), establishing a new paradigm for full-space electromagnetic field manipulation and wave-domain signal processing. By extending conventional planar stacked metasurfaces into a concentric spherical architecture, the S-SIM enables isotropic field interaction and full-space coverage. This article first establishes a unified modeling framework for S-SIM that integrates the architectural design, full-space wave-domain signal formulation, and channel representation to characterize inter-layer wave interactions and propagation characteristics. Building on this foundation, an iterative layer- by-layer beamforming algorithm is developed to optimize the phase responses of meta-atoms across multiple layers. Furthermore, a user-centric node division multiple access (NDMA) scheme is developed, allowing adaptive multiuser access and interference management in distributed three-dimensional environments. Case studies demonstrate that the proposed S-SIM system achieves more uniform full-space coverage and higher average spectral efficiency compared with the state-of-the-art (SoA) planar SIM. Finally, several open research directions are discussed, including hardware implementation, wave-domain computing, cooperative networking, and intelligent optimization, which lay the foundation on the practical deployment of S-SIM systems.

title: ChainVerFed: A Trustworthy Heterogeneous Federated Learning Framework with Chained Verification
authors: Jianbin Mai, Zhiquan Liu, Jingjing Guo, Yinbin Miao, Leping Li, Feixiang Ye
abstract: Federated learning (FL) for Consumer Electronics (CE) and consumer IoMT devices faces severe internal trust deficits. These are amplified in CE-centric IoMT deployments by model heterogeneity, data heterogeneity, and stringent privacy demands, manifesting as tamper-prone heterogeneous update histories, opaque and unauditable client aggregation, and inadequate resilience to malicious attacks. We propose ChainVerFed, a trustworthy FL framework for CE and consumer IoMT that incorporates chain-based verification via three innovations: Hierarchical Chain-aligned Latent Autoencoder (HCLAE), Hierarchical Dynamic Chain Clustering Attention (HDCCA), and Dual Exponential Chain Adaptive Privacy (DECAP). ChainVerFed delivers trustworthy, transparent, and secure collaboration across consumer IoMT devices common in CE scenarios. Experiments on consumer health monitoring datasets show consistent improvements over the compared methods, including up to 30.8% higher accuracy, 91.6% malicious-update filtering, and 2.9%–3.2% accuracy degradation under severe tampering. Theoretical analysis supports chain tamper detection, auditability, and attack robustness under the assumed threat model, underscoring Chain-VerFed’s potential for secure, verifiable learning in consumer electronics and consumer IoMT ecosystems.

title: A Mixed Reality System for Robust Manikin Localization in Childbirth Training
authors: Haojie Cheng, Chang Liu, Abhiram Kanneganti, Mahesh Arjandas Choolani, Arundhati Tushar Gosavi, Eng Tat Khoo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Opportunities for medical students to gain practical experience in vaginal births are increasingly constrained by shortened clinical rotations, patient reluctance, and the unpredictable nature of labour. To alleviate clinicians' instructional burden and enhance trainees' learning efficiency, we introduce a mixed reality (MR) system for childbirth training that combines virtual guidance with tactile manikin interaction, thereby preserving authentic haptic feedback while enabling independent practice without continuous on-site expert supervision. The system extends the passthrough capability of commercial head-mounted displays (HMDs) by spatially calibrating an external RGB-D camera, allowing real-time visual integration of physical training objects. Building on this capability, we implement a coarse-to-fine localization pipeline that first aligns the maternal manikin with fiducial markers to define a delivery region and then registers the pre-scanned neonatal head within this area. This process enables spatially accurate overlay of virtual guiding hands near the manikin, allowing trainees to follow expert trajectories reinforced by haptic interaction. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that the system achieves accurate and stable manikin localization on a standalone headset, ensuring practical deployment without external computing resources. A large-scale user study involving 83 fourth-year medical students was subsequently conducted to compare MR-based and virtual reality (VR)-based childbirth training. Four senior obstetricians independently assessed performance using standardized criteria. Results showed that MR training achieved significantly higher scores in delivery, post-delivery, and overall task performance, and was consistently preferred by trainees over VR training. Although validated only in the context of obstetric delivery, the system demonstrates strong potential for broader manikin-based procedural training and other healthcare education scenarios.

title: Physics-Based Reverse Recovery Modeling of the Internal Reverse Diode in SiC MOSFET
authors: Chihiro Kawahara, Yasushige Mukunoki, Tomohiro Tamaki, Takeshi Horiguchi, Shiro Hino, Tsunenobu Kimoto
abstract: This article presents a physics-based circuit model describing the reverse recovery behavior of the internal reverse diode [body diode (BD)] in silicon carbide (SiC) power MOSFET. The model considers physical properties of SiC over a wide operational temperature range (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$25~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$175~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C). The carrier distribution inside the BD is modeled by analytical equations, taking account of the injection efficiency of carriers as well as the effective carrier lifetime. This article also introduces a new technique for extracting the effective carrier lifetime of the device by measuring the voltage decay during the transition from the BD conduction to the reverse conduction of the MOSFET. The model accurately predicts reverse recovery behavior, validated by a comparison of the experimental and simulation results. This model enables circuit designers to optimize switching conditions for various applications, leading to a more efficient and reliable circuit design.

title: Stabilizing a linear system using phone calls when time is information
authors: Mohammad Javad Khojasteh, Massimo Franceschetti, Gireeja Ranade
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Control Systems
abstract: We study stabilizing a scalar linear system over a “timing” channel, where information is communicated via the timestamps of transmitted symbols. Each symbol, sent from a sensor to a controller in a closed-loop system, is received after a random delay. The sensor encodes information in the waiting times between transmissions, and the controller decodes it using the inter-reception times of symbols. This setup resembles a telephone system: a transmitter signals a call with a “ring,” and the receiver becomes aware of the “ring” after a random connection delay. With no data payload exchanged, this setup provides an abstraction for performing event-triggering control with zero-payload rate. We show that for system stabilization (state converging to zero in probability), the timing capacity of the channel must be at least as large as the entropy rate of the system. For exponentially distributed symbol delays, we provide an “almost” tight sufficient condition using a coding strategy that refines the message estimate with each new symbol. Our results generalize previous zero-payload event-triggering control strategies, revealing a fundamental limit for using timing information in stabilization, independent of the transmission strategy.

title: CascadeOcc: Rethinking 3D Occupancy World Models With Cascaded VQ Representations
authors: Kyumin Hwang, Wonhyeok Choi, Jaeyeul Kim, Jihun Park, Daehee Park, Sunghoon Im
abstract: This letter proposes CascadeOcc, a novel occupancy world model that prioritizes intrinsic structural hierarchy over extrinsic auxiliary modalities for autonomous driving. Occupancy world models—forecasting the future driving environment and planning the driving trajectory—effectively bridge perception and planning, but current approaches often heavily rely on external modalities or large language models, failing to fully exploit the inherent structural potential of occupancy representations themselves. To enhance representational capacity for complex 3D scenes, we integrate a cascaded Vector Quantized (VQ) mechanism into an autoregressive framework. Following a coarse-to-fine principle, CascadeOcc progressively refines fine-grained details from global structures through a multi-scale architecture. Additionally, we incorporate a TimeMixer to capture multi-scale temporal dependencies, establishing a dual-hierarchy mechanism in both space and time. Experimental results on 4D occupancy forecasting and motion planning benchmarks demonstrate that CascadeOcc achieves superior performance among vision-centric approaches, validating that optimizing inherent representations is a powerful alternative to relying on external foundation models.

title: Call for Nominations for Editor-in-Chief
authors: N/A

title: Traceable and Revocable Dynamic Searchable Symmetric Encryption with Forward Privacy and Conjunctive Query for Internet of Vehicles
authors: Liqing Chen, Yeting Wu, Hao Zhang, Jian Weng, Zhiquan Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: To achieve the sharing of traffic data with other vehicles, the related data of the Internet of Vehicles (IoV) normally have to be uploaded to the cloud server. Dynamic searchable symmetric encryption (DSSE) empowers users to perform retrieve operations on encrypted databases preserved on cloud servers and supports dynamic data updates at the same time. However, most of the DSSE solutions that support forward privacy and only supply single-keyword search, which places a limit on the practical implementations. When the data owner discloses data to shared users, licensed users might sell their private keys to achieve economic benefits. Based on the aforementioned issues, we put forward traceable and revocable dynamic searchable symmetric encryption with forward privacy and conjunctive query (TR-DSSE-FC), this scheme supports efficient conjunctive-query and the tracking and revocation of malicious users while ensuring forward privacy. This scheme realizes the conjunctive query function by integrating the inverted index with the forward index. The inverted index is deployed to extract the documents that align the least frequent items, while the forward index constructed based on t-puncturable pseudorandom functions (t-Pun-PRFs) is responsible for determining the final query results. To verify whether the search results generated by the cloud server have been modified, we design an accumulative verification label based on symmetric cryptography. Furthermore, we prove the security of TR-DSSE-FC through simulation experiments. According to the performance experiment analysis, compared with analogous approaches, TR-DSSE-FC has significant merits in conjunctive query and verification.

title: A Multi-Channel Time-Multiplexed Chopper Instrumentation Amplifier for Biopotential and Electrode-Tissue Impedance Measurement
authors: Ehsan Mohammadi, Mohammad Mahdi Moeini-Manesh, Ehsan Hadizadeh Hafshejani, Ali Fotowat-Ahmady
abstract: 3-channel implementation of the Time-Multiplexed Chopper Instrumentation Amplifier (TMCIA) is presented. A single amplification chain is shared across all channels. Electrode-Tissue Impedance (ETI) measurement for motion artifact detection is done by current generators injecting excitation currents, resulting in a frequency-multiplexed signal comprising both biosignal and ETI. The circuit operates in three stages: (1) a time-domain multiplexer chops and combines inputs; (2) a Current Feedback Instrumentation Amplifier (CFIA) performs amplification; and (3) a demulti-plexer separates the output into three channels. Chopping is applied at both input and output to suppress flicker noise and offset. Fabricated in a 0.18 μm CMOS process, the design occupies 0.028mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> per channel and consumes 1.81 μA (amplifier) and 0.2 μA (maximum per current generator). The circuit achieves a CMRR > 80 dB, with Noise Efficiency Factor (NEF) = 2.39 and Power Efficiency Factor (PEF) = 10.27.

title: A Generalized and Robust Online SOH Estimation Framework for Lithium-ion Batteries under Dynamic Conditions
authors: Shushan Qiu, Kaushik Rajashekara, Yingpei Li, Sai Bhargava Althurthi
abstract: Advanced data-driven state-of-health (SOH) estimation methods mainly rely on electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) to capture internal impedance characteristics. However, most online data-driven methods are trained on highly controlled laboratory datasets with standardized fixtures and steady charging protocols. When applied in real-world settings involving hardware-induced variations and dynamic charging profiles that alter the measured impedance, systematic errors emerge, substantially limiting their generalization capability. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a generalized and robust online SOH estimation framework for practical operating conditions. A hybrid physics–data-driven approach is adopted, where impedance features are first projected to eliminate resistive and inductive parasitic effects, and an Equivalent Circuit Model (ECM) is further integrated to decouple intrinsic degradation mechanisms. By extracting degradation-sensitive features, the model ensures that the SOH estimation remains physically interpretable and resilient to the different electrochemical responses. Experimental results demonstrate the framework's superior generalization capability, maintaining a maximum error of 4% over the entire aging trajectory on unseen datasets with varying impedance features, with errors reducing to below 0.5% under specific aging stages. Furthermore, the proposed method is validated on embedded platforms, completing impedance measurement and SOH estimation within 60 s with a deviation of less than 4%. This work bridges the gap between laboratory-grade analysis and practical application, offering a generalized, rapid, and physically interpretable solution for online SOH monitoring.

title: Topology Linearization for Multi-Agent Systems Security: Mitigating Malicious Propagation via Path Decomposition
authors: Yan Liu, Sujie Shao, Shaoyong Guo, Chenyu Wang, Chao Yang, Zhibin Zang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: The rapid adoption of Large Language Model (LLM)-based Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) is hindered by severe security risks, where high-connectivity topologies facilitate the explosive propagation of prompt injections and hallucinations. To address this, we propose the Chain-Type Secure Path Reconstruction Method (C-SPRM), a topological defense framework that fundamentally constrains threat lateral movement by transforming arbitrary interaction graphs into linearized logical execution chains based on path decomposition theory. We formulate the topology reconstruction as a Spatio-Temporal Attack Surface (STAS) minimization problem and develop a Graph-to-Sequence Path Decomposition via Reinforcement Learning (G2S-PDR) model to generate optimized execution sequences that balance concurrency risks against latency. Additionally, an adaptive topology virtualization mechanism is introduced to resolve topological bottlenecks in high-degree networks like star or fully connected graphs. Experimental results demonstrate that C-SPRM significantly reduces the effective blast radius of malicious agents and exponentially increases the difficulty of attack propagation, achieving superior system robustness with controllable computational overhead compared to traditional interaction architectures.

title: A Complex Vector-Based Uncertainty and Disturbance Estimation Control Strategy for PMSM Drives in Electric Power Steering
authors: Yuxiang Xue, Qishui Zhong, Yingzhe Wu, Hengbin Zhang, Xi Liu, Yishan Cai, Lisheng Wang, Hui Li, Xinyu Zhang
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: In this paper, a complex vector-based uncertainty and disturbance estimator (CVUDE) current control algorithm for permanent magnet synchronous machines (PMSMs) applied in electric power steering (EPS) is proposed. Firstly, the mathematical modeling of the motor drive incorporating the CVUDE strategy is derived. Then, the frequency domain analysis is carried out, which demonstrates that the CVUDE strategy can effectively eliminate static disturbances caused by the cross coupling terms and parameter mismatches, and suppress dynamic disturbances induced by the inverter nonlinearity compared with its proportional-integral (PI) counterparts. Meanwhile, a comprehensive control parameters tuning guideline has been established based on the frequency domain analysis of the closed current control loop and disturbance rejection function. Experimental results explicitly prove that the CVUDE control method presents an excellent control performance in terms of eliminating the adverse effects induced by static disturbances. In addition, it is demonstrated that the high-order current harmonics caused by dynamic disturbances can be effectively suppressed based on the CVUDE. According to comparisons with other advanced control methods, it is confirmed that the CVUDE strategy can ensure a good dynamic disturbance rejection capability across a wide speed range without a complicated computation process.

title: Interference suppression in 3D monopulse radar using external antenna elements.
authors: Pavel Bezoušek, Simeon Karamazov
abstract: This article presents a method for suppressing incoherent narrowband interference in a 3D surveillance radar with a multi-beam monopulse antenna by adapting the antenna pattern using external suppression antenna elements. The robust adaptation method mitigates the cancellation of the desired signal (DSC) through linear conditions on the weights of suppression elements. This method ensures that adaptation is not applied in selected directions. To strengthen the protection of the signal of interest (SOI) in the near vicinity of the monopulse channel axis (the so-called protected area), we introduce the division of the group of suppression elements into disjoint subgroups. The requirements are then applied to each subgroup separately. However, this step increases the number of conditions imposed on the weights of external elements, and thus partially reduces the quality of interference suppression outside the protected area. Therefore, the optimal number of conditions is a compromise depending on the total number of suppression elements and other parameters. The article describes a computer model of this adaptation method, and its functionality is verified by simulations. The optimal numbers of selected directions and groups of suppression elements for specific numbers of suppression elements were determined.

title: A Comprehensive Evaluation of Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning on Code Smell Detection
authors: Beiqi Zhang, Peng Liang, Xin Zhou, Xiyu Zhou, David Lo, Qiong Feng, Zengyang Li, Lin Li
publish: ACM Transactions on Software Engineering and Methodology
abstract: None

title: Experimental Design and Performance Simulation of Core Components for BBM92 Entanglement-Based QKD
authors: Viviana Cadena, Arifur R. Khan, Miralem Mehic, Jesus R. Lopez, Saeefa Rubaiyet Nowmi, Samee U. Khan, Mohammad Saidur Rahman
abstract: Entanglement-based quantum key distribution (QKD) offers robust key distribution through intrinsically correlated measurements, ensuring any eavesdropping attempt introduces detectable disturbances. Although practical implementations are primarily limited to fixed fiber networks, extending QKD to mobile platforms like drones or small satellites introduces challenges in optical alignment, weight, and power consumption. This work outlines the core components and architecture for implementing BBM92-based QKD in mobile settings. A compact polarization Sagnac interferometer utilizing a PPKTP crystal provides a stable and efficient method for generating entangled photon pairs. Supporting subsystems include a polarization compensation system, a detector module, and an acquisition, pointing, and tracking (APT) system. To assess performance under realistic conditions, we simulate photon loss, detector efficiency, and quantum bit error rates using the SQUANCH framework. Results show an average key generation rate of 5.43 bits/s, a final secure key length of 45.27 bits per block, and a QBER of 4.5%.

title: Miniaturized Multibeam Reflectarray Antenna Based on Mathematical Unit Cell Design
authors: Ali Ghadimi, Fadhel M. Ghannouchi, Mohammad S. Sharawi, Elham Baladi
abstract: This paper presents a novel mathematical approach for the design of miniaturized reflectarray antenna elements. The unit cell geometry is generated analytically using combined trigonometric and logarithmic functions, allowing systematic and continuous control of the reflection phase through a single geometrical tuning parameter, α. The proposed unit cell is implemented on a single-layer RO4003 substrate with a thickness of 1.57mm and a periodicity of 0.26λ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</sub> at 10 GHz, corresponding to a 1.92× size reduction relative to conventional 0.5λ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</sub> reflectarray (RA) unit cells. By varying α, a reflection phase range of 352° is achieved with an average reflection loss of approximately 0.2 dB. This compactness supports dense element integration, increasing the number of elements across the aperture from 104 to 424 and reducing the phase quantization step from 6.11° to 3.18°, leading to smoother aperture phase distributions and reduced phase errors. These characteristics also contribute to improved phase stability of the unit cell under oblique incidence angles up to 30°. A single-feed multibeam RA antenna is synthesized and evaluated numerically and experimentally based on the proposed unit cell using the addition theorem, showing a decent performance balance between the four intended beams and low sidelobes, owed to the compact unit cell geometry and smooth phase profile on the surface of the RA. The measurements show peak gains ranging from 15.33 to 16.84 dBi, and the measured 3 dB gain bandwidths varying between 7.44% and 15.32% over the 9–11 GHz frequency band. The measured results are in good agreement with full-wave simulations, validating the theoretical and numerical analyses.

title: A short-term EV charging load forecast method based on Kolmogorov–Arnold Spatiotemporal Attention Recurrent Network
authors: Mingyang Tu, Jia Liu, Zao Tang, Tong Su, Pingliang Zeng
abstract: Short-term electric vehicle charging load forecasting is essential for distribution grid operation, charging service planning, and near-term resource coordination. This paper proposes a Kolmogorov–Arnold Spatiotemporal Attention Recurrent Network (KASTAR) for multi-target forecasting in urban charging systems. The model combines a cross-correlation-guided graph attention encoder to capture meaningful spatial dependencies among charging zones with a gated recurrent decoder enhanced by Kolmogorov–Arnold temporal-pattern attention to model nonlinear lag effects. A lightweight Kolmogorov–Arnold output head is further used to generate multi-horizon forecasts while preserving interpretable shape functions. Experiments on multiple city-scale real-world datasets show that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art accuracy for 1–4 h-ahead forecasting. Compared with a structurally similar spatiotemporal baseline, KASTAR improves forecasting accuracy by 10.5% while using more than 50% fewer parameters. The model also maintains strong robustness under additive noise and missing data. Ablation and basis-replacement studies show that the temporal-pattern attention design and the localized B-spline basis are the main sources of the performance gains. These results demonstrate that KASTAR is an accurate, robust, and interpretable solution for short-term electric vehicle charging load forecasting.

title: Lightweight Cluster Matching-based Tracking Method for Fall Detection using a Single-LiDAR
authors: Gyu-Yeon Kim, Somi Park, Sunkyung Lee, Bobin Seo, Seon Han Choi, Sung Min Park
abstract: Falls are a major health risk in super-aged societies, particularly for elderly individuals living alone. Light detection and ranging (LiDAR) sensors have recently attracted attention for fall detection due to their robustness to lighting conditions and inherent privacy preservation. However, practical in-home deployment requires continuous operation under limited computational resources. This paper proposes a lightweight fall detection method using a single LiDAR sensor, integrating cluster matching-based tracking with rule-based acceleration analysis. The proposed approach enables continuous monitoring without multi-sensor configurations or learning-based models. Experimental results across diverse fall and non-fall scenarios demonstrate an accuracy of 89.2% and a sensitivity of 98.3%, while requiring only 1.02 MFLOPs per frame. The results confirm the feasibility of reliable and computationally efficient fall detection using a single LiDAR sensor.

title: Enhancing Deepfake Detection: Leveraging StyleGAN3 for Robust AI-Generated Forgery Identification
authors: Mohammad Hafezi, Essa Q. Shahra, Shadi Basurra, Adel Aneiba, Jack Devey
abstract: The rapid advancement of generative models has significantly increased the realism of AI-generated Deepfake content, posing serious challenges to digital media integrity and forensic analysis. A key difficulty in Deepfake detection lies in achieving robust generalization when confronted with synthetic images generated by previously unseen models that exhibit reduced visual artifacts. This study investigates the effectiveness of augmenting training data with StyleGAN3-generated images to enhance the generalization capability of Deepfake detection systems. Unlike earlier generative models, StyleGAN3 mitigates common artifacts such as texture sticking and aliasing, producing highly realistic synthetic faces that better represent modern forgery characteristics.We train a convolutional neural network (ResNet-18) under two controlled conditions: using a standard Deepfake dataset and using a dataset augmented with StyleGAN3-generated images. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed augmentation strategy yields a 20.5% absolute improvement in test accuracy, along with a substantial increase in true positive rate and a significant reduction in false negatives. These findings indicate that exposure to more realistic synthetic samples enables the model to learn deeper and more transferable representations of manipulated content. However, the improvement in fake detection performance is accompanied by a moderate rise in false positives, highlighting an important trade-off that must be considered in practical deployment. Overall, this work demonstrates that incorporating artifact-reduced synthetic images during training can improve the robustness of Deepfake detection models. The study contributes to ongoing efforts in digital media forensics by emphasizing the importance of realistic data augmentation strategies for strengthening detection systems against evolving generative techniques.

title: K-Means and Gaussian Mixture Models on Lie Groups: Application to Geometrical Clustering_supp1-3678342.pdf
authors: Samy LABSIR
abstract: This paper investigates two new clustering algorithms dedicated to Lie groups, adapted from the well known K-Means algorithm and Gaussian mixture models. More precisely, the K-Means algorithm is generalized to handle observations belonging to a Lie group (LG) with an appropriate intrinsic metric. New Gaussian mixture models are investigated for data living in LGs by using concentrated Gaussian distributions. The consistency and the performance of the resulting clustering methods are numerically validated for data lying on the LG SE(2), by comparison with the state of the art for synthetic data, pseudo-real data generated using an ultrasound sensor model and real data from the MNIST dataset.

title: Differential Privacy on Federated Learning: A Correlated Equilibrium and Simulated Annealing Approach
authors: Sávio Miranda, Luiz Moreira, Aruzhan Sabyrbek, André Riker, Eirini Eleni Tsiropoulou
abstract: Malicious agents can break data privacy of the federated learning users accessing their shared machine learning parameters. To address this problem, this paper proposes a solution, named Differential PRIvacy with buDget Equilibrium (D-PRIDE), that dynamically computes the Differential Privacy budget jointly based Correlated Equilibrium (CE) and Simulated Annealing for grouped FL clients. The CE enables DP budgets recommendation for the clients groups. Simulated Annealing and trimming is applied on the candidate budget values to navigate and reduce the search space for nearby budgets, ensuring a fast and more granular balance of privacy and utility. The proposed solution was tested on real-world datasets, where it was shown superior performance (e.g., accuracy and training time) compared to existing state-of-the-art approaches.

title: The Impact of Artificial Intelligence on Revenue Growth of Small Business
authors: Prithvi Sairaj Krishnan
abstract: With today's business world changing so quickly, artificial intelligence (AI) has become an increasingly crucial tool for enterprises of all kinds. This study set out to investigate the effects of AI applications on the revenue growth of small enterprises in developing nations through empirical testing. The study employed multiple regression analysis to examine the association between revenue growth and AI applications, utilizing data gathered from 391 small firms. AI-based customer service, marketing and advertising, sales forecasting, inventory management, personnel management, cybersecurity, financial planning and management, and lead generation were the study's independent variables. With the exception of AI-based employee management and cybersecurity companies, all of the independent factors were statistically significant, according to the analysis's findings. The study's conclusions underlined the value of using AI in small firms and made the case that such applications may boost revenue development. The results revealed that AI applications in Customer Financial planning, inventory management, sales forecasting, marketing, and advertising & management, as well as Lead Generation, significantly influenced the rise of revenue. This study adds to the body of knowledge by presenting actual data demonstrating the advantages of AI for emerging nations' small enterprises. The study's findings can help guide the tactics of little enterprises and decision-makers in poor nations and assist them in better understanding the part AI plays in helping small firms expand. The survey emphasizes how small firms must spend money on AI technologies to boost their bottom line and stay competitive. The study's findings highlight the value of using AI in small enterprises and imply that AI programs may boost revenue development. The results highlight how small firms must make investments in AI technology to boost their bottom line and stay competitive.

title: StickerShop: A Python Shopping Cart Program
authors: Agneya Dutta Pooleery
abstract: StickerShop is a Python program which allows users to buy stickers and other items/merchandise at their leisure. The program is designed to be used as a fundraiser for the Gifted Math Program (GMP) at the State University of New York at Buffalo. In the program, a user can register and login; they are then presented with a menu where they can see what stickers and other items/merchandise are available for purchase. The program also includes a shopping cart, where users can add or delete items from their cart, check individual item prices, and compute the total price of all the items they wish to purchase. The program utilizes many different Python commands and features and demonstrates a fluent use of the coding language. We hope that a portion of the proceeds from our sale of stickers and other items can be handed over to the GMP program at UB to support various club activities (such as "Math problem solving and pizza night", 3D printing initiatives, video game design). In the future, we hope that StickerShop will develop into a GitHub based web application which would allow users to register and sign in, and then browse the website to make purchases. Transactions will be stored in a database at the back end, and we will hook-up the application with a payment management portal.

title: Drones as a Platform for Integrated STEM Education: Bridging Engineering, Programming, and Real-World Applications
authors: Buyan Danzyuryun
abstract: The rapid evolution of unmanned aerial vehicles (drones) is revolutionizing various industrial applications, and their integration into education presents a unique opportunity for comprehensive STEM learning. This paper proposes a curriculum framework that harnesses drones as a versatile platform for integrated STEM education, bridging engineering design, computer programming, and real-world applications. Drawing inspiration from two industrial research projects—one on autonomous drone-based inspection systems for thermal power plant boilers and another on generating synthetic data using digital twins of industrial instruments—we demonstrate how advanced technologies can be adapted for classroom use. Our curriculum is structured into sequential modules that guide students through the process of assembling a modular drone kit, programming autonomous navigation in confined and challenging environments, and implementing computer vision algorithms for defect detection. In doing so, learners engage with fundamental STEM concepts such as aerodynamics, sensor integration, control systems, and machine learning. This hands-on, project-based approach not only cultivates technical proficiency and interdisciplinary problem solving but also fosters critical thinking, creativity, and collaborative skills among students. Furthermore, by simulating real-world industrial challenges, the framework provides educators with a robust model for professional development and curriculum integration. Ultimately, this integrated approach highlights the transformative potential of drone technology to make STEM education more engaging, relevant, and aligned with current industry practices, thereby preparing students to meet future technological challenges.

title: Formal Modeling and Verification of Block Production BABE Protocol in Polkadot
authors: Muhammad Rashid, Nazir Ahmad Zafar, Waheed Shahzad, Lal Khan, Mohammad Zubair Khan, Ibrahim Aljubayri
abstract: Polkadot is an emerging blockchain application in the cryptocurrency ecosystem, where a large number of participants actively take part to earn rewards. Block production in Polkadot is one of the most critical and technically complex components, which is governed by the Blind Assignment for Blockchain Extension (BABE) protocol. In BABE, validators compete in time slots to produce blocks by following the longest chain rule. To determine the eligibility of a specific validator for a given slot, several sub-operations are performed, including block lottery execution, block authoring, and block building procedures. Even a minor flaw in these processes can adversely affect the correctness of the chain and may compromise the overall blockchain operation. To ensure a reliable and error-free system, rigorous formal verification is essential to mathematically guarantee system correctness and reliability. Existing studies mainly rely on informal testing or limited assertion-based validation, while a comprehensive formal verification of BABE is still missing. To bridge this gap, this work presents a formal verification of the BABE protocol using model checking techniques. For this purpose, an integrated behavioral model of BABE is developed using the Process Meta Language (Promela), in which each core operational aspect of the protocol is modeled in detail. After modeling, key safety and liveness properties, such as fairness, availability, integrity, and consistency, are formally specified using Linear Temporal Logic (LTL). Both the Promela model and the corresponding LTL specifications are provided as input to the model checker, which verifies the properties over all possible executions of the model. Additionally, verification results include an analysis of the explored state space, verification time, and memory consumption. This study contributes toward the formal verification of other major Polkadot protocols by demonstrating the applicability of model checking, specifically using the SPIN model checker.

title: Computational Modeling and Optimization of Large-area Showerhead Configurations in PECVD Reactors
authors: Emad Barakat, Yeonchan Kim, Joo Pyo Hong, Seungkyung Park
abstract: This Plasma enhanced chemical vapor deposition (PECVD), a key process in semiconductor manufacturing, typically requires optimization of device geometry and operating conditions to achieve uniform deposition across large substrates, ensuring high-quality production. Computational fluid dynamics (CFD) serves as a critical tool for simulating and designing flow fields; however, the complexity of device geometries, particularly in large-scale PECVD reactors with thousands to hundreds of thousands of nozzles, often poses significant challenges to numerical modeling due to substantial computational cost. In this study, we propose a novel series porous resistance model (SPRM) that significantly reduces computational cost by representing the complex nozzle geometry as simplified porous media. The SPRM provides an analogous mathematical representation for each individual nozzle without requiring the actual geometric modeling, thereby enabling efficient simulation and optimization of nozzle arrays without recreating a mesh model for each design case. The efficiency of the proposed SPRM is demonstrated through 28 distinct showerhead configurations, with flow fields and deposition characteristics evaluated and compared. The results show that the SPRM achieves up to a 90% reduction in computational load compared to conventional CFD approaches and eliminates the need for remeshing during optimization.
