title: Retrieving Ice Water Path using GMI brightness temperature data based on the new-constructed ARM Database
authors: Yanting Wu, Zhenzhan Wang, Wenyu Wang
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Using neural network to retrieve ice water path (IWP) from spaceborne microwave radiometer brightness temperatures is a new technical method for remote sensing ice cloud microphysical parameters, but constructing a high-quality retrieval database remains a key limitation to its application. To this end, this study proposes a database construction method driven by the “All-Day Radiance Matching IWP Construction” algorithm. This algorithm is used to extend the spatial coverage of CloudSat-derived IWP from the satellite track to both sides of the orbit. Designed for both day and night conditions, this algorithm aims to cover the swath range of the Global Precipitation Measurement (GPM) Microwave Imager (GMI). Based on this database, the IWP retrieval algorithm built with the neural network has the following performance metrics: a root mean square error (RMSE) of 610.41 g/m², a mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of 48.7%, a coefficient of determination of 0.729, and a Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.855. The results indicate that the neural network model established using the constructed database can effectively and stably retrieve the IWP from the GMI data.

title: Digital literacy and AI in underfunded schools targeting school teachers (Diversity and inclusion)
authors: Catherine Beation
abstract: Learning Object: – Educators will leave the workshop with a strong foundation in understanding AI. Educators will develop skills in generating and customizing AI input to develop accessible and neutral educational modules for students. – Educators will have an understanding of the free/affordable resources they can use in the classroom.

title: Split Learning-Based Channel Prediction for 6G-Enabled LEO Satellite Systems
authors: Kithmini Weththasinghe, Quynh Tu Ngo, Ying He, Beeshanga Jayawickrama
publish: IEEE Transactions on Green Communications and Networking
abstract: In sixth-generation communications, non-terrestrial networks provide scalable solutions to bridge connectivity gaps, particularly in areas where terrestrial infrastructure is lacking. Low Earth orbit (LEO) satellites are central to this effort due to their reduced signal propagation delays, which enable real-time, mission-critical communications, especially during emergencies when ground infrastructure is compromised. In such dynamic environments, accurate channel prediction is essential to maintaining consistent performance under varying conditions. However, conventional channel prediction methods that rely on uplink-to-downlink reciprocity, common in terrestrial time-division duplexing systems, are ineffective in LEO scenarios due to rapid channel variations, Doppler shifts, and channel aging. These challenges are intensified during unpredictable environmental conditions, such as natural disasters. To address this, we propose a hybrid offline-online learning framework for real-time downlink channel prediction based on uplink data in LEO systems. This approach enables an adaptive response to dynamic communication conditions. Additionally, a split learning strategy distributes computational load between satellites and user equipment, avoiding overloading LEO platforms. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed model achieves lower normalised mean square error across various LEO configurations. The results validate the effectiveness of the proposed model under dynamic 6G channel conditions.

title: Decentralized Trust for Space AI: Blockchain-Based Federated Learning Across Multi-Vendor LEO Satellite Networks
authors: Mohamed Elmahallawy, Asma Jodeiri Akbarfam
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Big Data (BigData)
abstract: The rise of space AI is reshaping government and industry through applications such as disaster detection, border surveillance, and climate monitoring, powered by massive data from commercial and governmental low Earth orbit (LEO) satellites. Federated satellite learning (FSL) enables joint model training without sharing raw data, but suffers from slow convergence due to intermittent connectivity and introduces critical trust challenges—where biased or falsified updates can arise across satellite constellations, including those injected through cyberattacks on inter-satellite or satellite–ground communication links. We propose OrbitChain, a blockchain-backed framework that empowers trustworthy multi-vendor collaboration in LEO networks. OrbitChain (i) offloads consensus to high-altitude platforms (HAPs) with greater computational capacity, (ii) ensures transparent, auditable provenance of model updates from different orbits owned by different vendors, and (iii) prevents manipulated or incomplete contributions from affecting global FSL model aggregation. Extensive simulations show that OrbitChain reduces computational and communication overhead while improving privacy, security, and global model accuracy. Its permissioned proof-of-authority ledger finalizes over 1,000 blocks with sub-second latency (0.16,s, 0.26,s, 0.35,s for 1-of-5, 3-of-5, and 5-of-5 quorums). Moreover, OrbitChain reduces convergence time by up to 30 hours on real satellite datasets compared to single-vendor, demonstrating its effectiveness for real-time, multi-vendor learning. Our code is available at GitHub

title: RL-Based Anti-Jamming Maritime Communications for LLM Inference
authors: Huanhuan Liu, Liang Xiao, Weiqi Lin, Pengli Zhang, Haoyu Chen, Zefang Lv, Manhao Jiang
abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL)-based anti-jamming communication schemes select the transmit power and channel to send images or point clouds, but have low transmission reliability due to lack of maritime environmental features and the delayed feedback under harsh channel conditions caused by sea surface reflections and wave fluctuations. In this paper, we propose an RL-based anti-jamming maritime communication scheme for LLM inference that enables user equipment (UE) to optimize uplink power, channel and data compression ratio for transmitting multi-modal data based on data size of each modality, number of received packets, environmental features such as weather conditions and jamming features such as the received jamming power with quality-of-service guarantee. The historical anti-jamming experiences are used to recover the delayed feedback or loss and reduce communication outage under harsh maritime channel. The upper bound in terms of latency, UE energy consumption and inference accuracy is provided based on a maritime anti-jamming game to show the impact of the number of modalities, bandwidth and channel gains. The proposed scheme is implemented based on UEs equipped with Jetson Orin, camera and BME280 sensors for transmitting 200-KB temperature, humidity and pressure as well as images to the control center for LLaVA-based environmental feature extraction and marine object detection. Experimental results based on 4 UEs at Harbor show the performance gain with 40.5% less latency, 46.4% lower UE energy consumption and 10.9% higher inference accuracy against the smart jammer.

title: Exploring Spatial Stratified Heterogeneity Patterns in Spatial Data: Balancing Heterogeneity with Stratification Complexity
authors: Hexiang Bai, Zilong Yang, Jianlong Hu, Qian Chen, Sichen Liu, Deyu Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Spatial stratified heterogeneity refers to the pattern variation of the target phenomenon across different regions. Current measures mainly quantify spatially stratified heterogeneity in terms of the consistency between strata and the target variable and neglect the complexity of stratification, whereas complex stratification may lead to overfitting and an overestimated degree of heterogeneity. To address this issue, this paper enhances the relative-entropy-based spatial stratified heterogeneity measure to unit explanatory power using the entropy of the stratification. The proposed method first quantifies the stratification complexity using the minimum theoretical number of bits for encoding it, then characterizes the degree of heterogeneity per bit, i.e., unit explanatory power, by the quotient of the relative-entropy-based measure and the stratification complexity. Additionally, this paper develops two visualization tools for interpreting and comparing unit explanatory power and reveals the relation among spatial stratified heterogeneity, stratification complexity, and the log-likelihood function. Finally, we conduct experiments on both illustrative and real-life data sets to show the advantages of the unit explanatory power over traditional spatial stratified heterogeneity measures. The code for computing unit explanatory power has been released at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/crafly/ssh_with_gran</uri>.

title: GAMED: Gamification for Engineering Design and Problem-Solving
authors: Caitlin Johnson, Mehdi Roopaei
abstract: By the end of this workshop, participants will: • Understand the Strategic Gamification Framework and how it enhances STEM project engagement, problem-solving, and engineering design. • Explore how to structure STEM projects as quests, incorporating epic missions, challenges, progress tracking, and feedback loops. • Identify different student player types (Dominator, Achiever, Connector, Explorer) and design activities that motivate diverse learners. • Develop a gamified STEM project prototype, applying game mechanics such as points, badges, levels, streaks, and team collaborations. • Learn how to integrate technology tools (AI-driven feedback, gamified platforms) to enhance hands-on learning.

title: Optimizing the Performances of Phosphorus Doped Optical Fiber Dosimeters Operating in the Visible Domain
authors: M. Roche, A. Morana, F. Fricano, N. Kerboub, J. Mekki, G. Mélin, T. Robin, E. Marin, A. Boukenter, Y. Ouerdane, D. Lambert, P. Paillet, S. Girard
abstract: We investigate the performances of a dosimeter exploiting the radiation induced attenuation (RIA) of phosphosilicate multimode optical fiber in the visible domain. For this, we investigate the visible RIA under various irradiation conditions: dose up to 15 Gy, dose rates from 675 μGy/s to 50 mGy/s and temperatures of irradiation from -40 °C to 80 °C. This study was supplemented by an extensive analysis of the effect of photobleaching on the RIA and its impact on the dosimetry applications. Using optical fibers with larger cores enable to mitigate the undesirable effects of photobleaching on dosimetry performances observed with single-mode fibers. The results have enabled the development of a mathematical tool for simulating fiber performance based on its intrinsic parameters and the dose deposited.

title: Editorial: Special Issue on Interactive Power Converters for Renewable Energy Grid-Tied Systems
authors: Giovanni De Carne, Georgios Konstantinou, Marta Molinas
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics

title: Dynamic Muscle State-Driven Framework for Intelligent Grading of Chronic Trapezius Fatigue
authors: Yujia Chen, Ling He, Guanci Yang, Dan Liu, Donghua Zheng, Lucheng Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Prolonged sedentary work often induces chronic shoulder muscle fatigue, which can lead to persistent pain. Objective quantification of cumulative fatigue severity is critical for effective intervention, yet existing methods lack standardized multidimensional assessment tools. Focusing on the trapezius, a key stabilizer of the shoulder girdle and a common site of pain, this study proposes a novel muscle mechanical state-based grading framework for chronic fatigue. Muscle tone and stiffness were used as dual biomarkers to characterize muscle rigidity. A two-phase experimental paradigm was designed to analyze the temporal evolution and correlation patterns of these two parameters during fatigue progression. Based on the dynamic regulatory capacity reflected in muscle mechanical state data, a multi-level grading system for chronic muscle fatigue was established. For intelligent assessment, three dual-branch graph-structured models were developed under a progressive framework of “independence → simple interaction → deep interaction.” The final HG-GCN model employed cross-attention to associate the spatiotemporal features of the middle and lower trapezius and dynamically fused features through a gated mechanism. The model achieved classification accuracies of 88.67% for the middle trapezius and 89.17% for the lower trapezius, demonstrating its effectiveness in detecting and quantifying chronic fatigue for early intervention.

title: Evolutionary Zero-Shot Proxy in Various Evaluation Scenarios: A Symbolic Learning Perspective
authors: Nan Li, Bing Xue, Qi Chen, Lianbo Ma, Min Huang, Xinwei Wang, Mengjie Zhang
abstract: Neural architecture search (NAS) faces a significant challenge due to the high computational cost of architecture evaluation. Zero-shot (ZS) proxies have been proposed as an efficient alternative to reduce evaluation overhead; however, their limited rank correlation often leads to sub-optimal architecture selection. To alleviate this issue, recent studies attempt to combine multiple ZS proxies, yet most existing approaches rely on heuristic aggregation guided by domain knowledge and remain restricted to a single evaluation scenario, which limits both effectiveness and interpretability. In this paper, we propose a novel perspective that treats ZS proxies as architectural features and formulates their combination as an evolutionary symbolic learning problem. Specifically, we leverage a genetic programming based symbolic learning framework to automatically evolve interpretable proxy combinations, enabling flexible exploration of symbolic structures without manual design. The evolved symbolic models capture complex and non-linear relationships between ZS proxies and architectural performance while maintaining strong interpretability. Furthermore, we systematically model three evaluation scenarios, including pointwise, pairwise, and listwise settings, and design scenario-specific fitness functions to guide evolutionary search toward different ranking objectives. Extensive experiments across 19 tasks using 13 representative ZS proxies are conducted to evaluate the proposed framework. The results demonstrate that the evolved symbolic models consistently achieve improved rank correlation and provide valuable insights into proxy interaction patterns under different evaluation scenarios, highlighting the effectiveness and generalizability of the proposed approach for NAS.

title: A 3D Cross-modal Keypoint Descriptor for MR-US Matching and Registration
authors: Daniil Morozov, Reuben Dorent, Nazim Haouchine
abstract: Intraoperative registration of real-time ultra-sound (iUS) to preoperative Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) remains an unsolved problem due to severe modality-specific differences in appearance, resolution, and field-of-view. To address this, we propose a novel 3D cross-modal keypoint descriptor for MRI–iUS matching and registration. Our approach employs a patient-specific matching-by-synthesis approach, generating synthetic iUS volumes from preoperative MRI. This enables supervised contrastive training to learn a shared descriptor space. A probabilistic keypoint detection strategy is then employed to identify anatomically salient and modality-consistent locations. During training, a curriculum-based triplet loss with dynamic hard negative mining is used to learn descriptors that are i) robust to iUS artifacts such as speckle noise and limited coverage, and ii) rotation-invariant. At inference, the method detects keypoints in MR and real iUS images and identifies sparse matches, which are then used to perform rigid registration. Our approach is evaluated using 3D MRI-iUS pairs from the ReMIND dataset. Experiments show that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art keypoint matching methods across 11 patients, with an average precision of 69.8%. For image registration, our method achieves a competitive mean Target Registration Error of 2.39 mm on the ReMIND2Reg benchmark. Compared to existing iUS-MR registration approaches, our framework is interpretable, requires no manual initialization, and shows robustness to iUS field-of-view variation. Code, data and model weights are available at https://github.com/ morozovdd/CrossKEY.

title: Toward Optical Switching and Routing in Non-Terrestrial Networks: Challenges and Opportunities
authors: Admela Jukan, Nikos Karafolas, Guray Acar, Aviv Guetta, Italo Brasileiro, Xavi Masip, Dominique Verchere
abstract: As the demand for ever more capacity, coverage and reach rapidly increases in next generation mobile networks, terrestrial fiber networks are envisioned to be increasingly deployed in tandem with the non-terrestrial networks, where the link transmission is based on free space optics (FSO), and their radio frequency link (RF) counterparts. On High Altitude Platform Systems (HAPSs), such as airships or balloons, it is expected that FSO and RF transmission technology are to be deployed in parallel based on weather. For inter-satellite communications, on the other hand, FSO links are likely to be a dominant communication link technology. All networking functions on-board a satellite, or HAPS, are however still implemented in electronics, including switching and routing. The next innovation frontier for non-terrestrial networking is to expanding its capabilities toward ultra-high-speed all-optical switching and routing, thus creating the optical NTN continuum. This article elaborates on state of the art in non-terrestrial optical networking, including HAPS and satellite, and discusses challenges and opportunities in control and data planes for spaceborne all-optical switching and routing. We provide an overview of related open-source efforts and share the vision of innovation through collaboration.

title: Taking Language Embedded 3D Gaussian Splatting into the Wild
authors: Yuze Wang, Junyi Wang, Yue Qi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Recent advances in leveraging large-scale Internet photo collections for 3D reconstruction have enabled immersive virtual exploration of landmarks and historic sites worldwide. However, existing methods primarily focus on visual appearance reconstruction, often overlooking the interactive semantic understanding of these 3D scenes (e.g., identifying specific building parts or scene details), which remains largely confined to browsing static text-image pairs. Therefore, can we draw inspiration from 3D in-the-wild reconstruction techniques and use unconstrained photo collections to create an immersive approach for comprehensive 3D scene understanding beyond mere visual appearance? To this end, we extend language embedded 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) and propose a novel framework for open-vocabulary scene understanding from unconstrained photo collections. Specifically, we first render multiple appearance images from the same viewpoint as the unconstrained image with the reconstructed radiance field, then extract multi-appearance CLIP features and two types of language feature uncertainty maps-transient and appearance uncertainty-derived from the multi-appearance features to guide the subsequent optimization process. Next, we propose a transient uncertainty-aware autoencoder, a multi-appearance language field 3DGS representation, and a post-ensemble strategy to effectively compress, learn, and fuse language features from multiple appearances. Finally, to quantitatively evaluate our method, we introduce PT-OVS, a new benchmark dataset for assessing open-vocabulary segmentation performance on unconstrained photo collections. Experimental results show that our method outperforms existing methods, delivering accurate open-vocabulary segmentation and enabling applications such as interactive roaming with open-vocabulary queries, architectural style pattern recognition, and 3D scene editing.

title: Research on Path Planning for Mobile Police Robots Based on Improved Crested Porcupine Optimizer and DWA Algorithm
authors: Dong Yu, Qilei Wang, Huitong Wang, Longbo Xu, Xinyang Wang, Yuchuan Zhang
abstract: To address challenges in path planning for police patrol robots operating in complex urban environments, particularly the tendency to become trapped in local optima and the limited capability for dynamic obstacle avoidance. This study proposes a hybrid path-planning framework that integrates a multi-strategy enhanced Crested Porcupine Optimizer algorithm with the Dynamic Window Approach. Specifically, Tent chaotic mapping is employed to improve population initialization, while adaptive control strategies are developed to balance exploration and exploitation. In addition, a butterfly-inspired search mechanism is introduced to strengthen global optimization performance. To enhance local obstacle avoidance and trajectory tracking, a global-path guidance module is embedded into DWA, enabling the local planner to follow the global route more reliably in cluttered and dynamic scenes. Simulation results indicate that the proposed approach outperforms baseline algorithms in terms of both convergence precision and reliability. Moreover, it generates smooth and safe patrol trajectories with improved responsiveness in both static and dynamic obstacle scenarios, thereby enhancing the autonomous patrol capability of robots in complex urban environments.

title: Synthesis of Noisy Sampled-data Networked Control Systems Under Deception Attacks: Direct Current Motor Applications
authors: Tianjiao Liu, Jianwei Xia, Guoliang Chen, Ju H. Park
abstract: The stabilization issue of noisy sampled-data networked control systems in the presence of deception attacks and noisy sampling intervals is investigated in this paper. Firstly, networked control systems with deception attacks and noisy sampling intervals are acquired under a consistent structure. Next, feedback interconnection model with zero initial state is established, which facilitates the use of the stochastic discrete-time integral quadratic constraint. Uniform exponential stability conditions are given for networked control systems related to operators expectations with stochastic properties. Subsequently, the difficulties associated with the nonlinear term are overcome and a novel controller algorithm that enables uniform exponential stability of the networked control systems is designed. Lastly, the effectiveness of the designed algorithm is validated by numerical example and direct current motor simulation example.

title: DAB-GRU: A Dynamic Attention-Based Bidirectional Network for Anomaly Detection of On-Board Time-Series Data in High-Speed Rail
authors: Wenbing Zhu, Ming Xu, Zhiyang Yu, Yang Wang, Xinjun Gao, Xiaoming Chen
abstract: The time-series data from the Chinese Train Control System (CTCS) is characterized by complex multidimensional correlations. Traditional statistical and machine learning methods are often limited by local statistical features, making it difficult to capture the associative logic within the data. Meanwhile, existing deep learning models relying on static context vectors suffer from information forgetting, hindering the precise identification of implicit anomalies. To address these issues, this paper proposes DAB-GRU, an unsupervised anomaly detection model. By combining a bidirectional GRU encoder with a dynamic attention mechanism, the model effectively extracts global temporal features to alleviate information forgetting and adaptively focuses on key feature points to capture implicit logical violations. Experimental results on the Guangzhou-Shenzhen-Hong Kong Express Rail Link high-speed rail data show that the proposed model achieves an average F1-score of 0.913 and an AUC of 0.969 across different anomaly scenarios. These results verify the model's significant advantages in overcoming feature bottlenecks and realizing the precise capture of contextual logical conflicts.

title: SSCS Arduino Contest: An International Not-For-Profit STEM Competition
authors: Jeffrey Wilcox, Jeremy Cosson-Martin, Kathryn Wilcox
abstract: As electronics become more ingrained in society through the use of phones, computers, and smart devices, accessible education in these areas is essential. Early involvement in science, technology, engineering, and math (STEM) programs is vital to reduce the gender gap and overcome cultural and economic barriers for students pursuing STEM careers. By organizing an international not-for-profit microcontroller-based competition complemented with tutorials, financial support, and dedicated volunteers, the IEEE Solid-State Circuits Society (SSCS) Education Committee has developed an inclusive platform that is accessible to students of all genders, ethnicities, and economic backgrounds. By marketing through social media platforms, the contest can reach more students at a significantly reduced cost, allowing more financial support to go directly to students. This scalable platform provides the means and motivation to explore electronics through Project Based Learning (PBL), an opportunity often unavailable through other channels. Compared to similar STEM programs, our initiative offers unique hands-on experiences, fostering creativity and problem-solving in a complementary way to existing STEM programs.

title: Real-Time Pothole Detection From Blurred Road Imagery with Deep Learning Networks
authors: Youngjae Sim

title: Betrayal!: Contending with Misalignments in Temporal Health Status Representations with Self-Tracked Data
authors: Adrienne Pichon, Lena Mamykina, Noémie Elhadad

title: An Integrated Broadband Lumped-Element Marchand Balun With Arbitrary Impedance Transformation and Arbitrary Coupling-Coefficient
authors: Kyutaek Oh, Ilku Nam, Ockgoo Lee
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: This letter introduces a broadband lumped-element Marchand balun (MB) with arbitrary impedance transformation and arbitrary inductive coupling-coefficient (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$k_{L}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>). Considering on-chip implementation, the lumped-element MB can replace bulky coupled transmission lines (TLs) of conventional coupled-TL MB. Furthermore, analytical expressions for the lumped-element counterparts have been derived for arbitrary <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$k_{L}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, independent of the impedance transformation ratio. By analyzing <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$k_{L}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, the lumped-element MB capable of operating in a broadband range can be designed. Based on these analyses, the proposed lumped-element MB was fabricated using a 65-nm bulk CMOS process. Measurement results demonstrate an insertion loss of less than 6 dB (including the 3-dB power splitting loss), an amplitude imbalance (AI) of less than 0.52 dB, and a phase imbalance (PI) (i.e., deviation from 180°) of less than 3.6° over a wide frequency range of 3–13 GHz.

title: Toward Robust IoT Device Authentication: Cross-Day Specific Emitter Identification via Domain Adaptation
authors: Qi Wu, Qun Wan, Guan Gui, Hien Quoc Ngo, Michail Matthaiou
abstract: Specific emitter identification (SEI) exploits device-dependent RF fingerprints to distinguish individual transmitters and is important for securing large-scale Internet-of-Things (IoT) deployments. While deep SEI can achieve near-perfect accuracy under same-day evaluation, real deployments rarely satisfy this assumption. At scale, per-day labeling is infeasible; models must therefore generalize from a labeled source day to an unlabeled target day, where day-to-day propagation drift induces distribution shifts and can substantially degrade performance under direct transfer (without adaptation). To address this challenge, we propose a unified unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) framework for cross-day SEI that requires neither hardware calibration nor handcrafted features. The proposed objective integrates adversarial domain alignment, confidence-aware pseudo-labeling to exploit high-confidence target samples safely, and a cross-domain contrastive regularizer to preserve class-discriminative geometry. We further provide an analysis offering insight into how each component contributes to target-domain generalization. Experiments on two public RF benchmarks from different wireless technologies demonstrate robust cross-day performance across diverse transfers. On WiSig–ManySig, our method achieves 99.78% mean cross-day accuracy over six source-to-target day transfers, ranking best in five cases and remaining within 0.04% of the best in the remaining case. On a LoRa benchmark, it achieves 90.64% mean cross-day accuracy over ten day-transfer pairs, validating the framework beyond Wi-Fi and under larger transfer diversity.

title: Forward-Error-Correction Dynamic Network for High-Accuracy Transmission in Disturbed Multimode Fiber
authors: Yang Hu, Minyu Fan, Kun Liu, Nan Jiang, Sha Wang
abstract: Lasers propagating through scattering media like multimode fibers (MMF) form distorted speckle patterns via multipath scattering, hindering signal recovery. Though data-driven deep learning has potential, neural networks cannot achieve 100% accuracy in noisy dynamic scattering media. This is due to irreversible physical information loss, incomplete training data for time-varying channels, and models lacking physical priors. To solve this, we propose a Forward-Error-Correction Dynamic Network (FECDN), fusing deep residual networks (ResNet) with Reed-Solomon (RS) coding. Guided by forward error correction in information theory, this framework leverages RS coding, a typical information-theoretic tool, to introduce redundancy at the transmitter and achieve real-time byte-level error correction at the receiver, fundamentally protecting the self-supervised training of the neural network from corrupted pseudo-labels, while ResNet captures transmission pattern changes with fewer samples to boost efficiency. Under a 100 m MMF with manual perturbations and without temperature control, the channel exhibits severe instability. In this scenario, the long-term structural similarity index (SSIM) dropped below 0.1 and the short-term SSIM dropped below 0.9. Despite these challenging conditions, FECDN achieved real-time transmission of H.265-compressed 200 × 200 video with zero frame loss and 100% symbol accuracy. This framework addresses instability and accumulation in dynamic scattering channels, providing a robust solution for real-time optical communication and laying the groundwork for intelligent, adaptive optical systems in complex or long-distance scenarios.

title: Evaluating Large Language Models on Named Entity Recognition
authors: Bin Ji, Huijun Liu, Shasha Li, Jun Ma, Jie Yu, See-Kiong Ng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are popping up all over the place, and they have been gaining prominence due to their exceptional abilities in conducting various tasks. Although extensive LLM evaluation has been explored on natural language understanding tasks like text classification and sentiment analysis, evaluating LLMs on named entity recognition (NER) still remains under-explored. To fill this gap, we evaluate twenty-eight representative LLMs on thirteen datasets across five domains, whose parameters range from 3 billion to 175billion, from four perspectives, that is, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), parameter scales, hallucinations, and prompt designs. We propose an LLM-based NER framework (LLM-NER) for the evaluation, which consists of a Recognition phase and a Check phase. Specifically, the Check guides LLMs to examine the correctness of recognized entities, which is designed to mitigate hallucinations in the NER scenario. Qualitative and quantitative evaluation analyses demonstrate that in the NER scenario: 1) SFT empowers LLMs to understand and follow human instructions; 2) LLMs’ ability generally improves as their parameter scales consistently increase; 3) hallucinations exist in all evaluated LLMs, and guiding LLMs to check their outputs is a feasible way to alleviate hallucinations; and 4) all evaluated LLMs are sensitive to prompt designs. Based on the analyses, we highlight a number of promising directions for future study. Moreover, our evaluation shows high consistency with two LLM evaluation leaderboards, which evaluate LLMs on other tasks, demonstrating the rationality of our evaluation design.

title: Joint Design of Intelligent Reflecting Surface Partitioning and Scheduling for Multiuser Communications
authors: Hiroaki Hashida, Taishi Endo, Yishi Zhu, Yuichi Kawamoto, Nei Kato, Takumi Yoneda, Tomoki Murakami
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: Intelligent reflecting surfaces (IRSs) have emerged as a promising solution to enhance wireless communication, particularly in high-frequency bands where signal attenuation and blockage are commonly encountered. However, owing to the passive and analog nature of IRS, generating beams that support multiple users (UEs) simultaneously is difficult, and conventional methods face limitations in scenarios requiring ultra-massive connectivity, such as 6G networks. To address this, we propose a multiaccess scheme based on IRS partitioning. The IRS is divided into multiple regions, each of which directs a beam to a specific UE. Our method jointly optimizes communication scheduling, partition positions, and phase offsets to improve performance. Numerical evaluations demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms traditional time-division multiple access (TDMA)-based IRS in terms of transmission. Furthermore, we examined the impact of each control parameter on performance, confirming that our method mitigates destructive signal superposition at partition boundaries and enhances communication efficiency. These findings suggest that the proposed IRS partitioning scheme is a viable approach to achieving ultra-high-speed, high-capacity, and ultra-massive connectivity in next-generation wireless networks.

title: Toward Real-Time Earth System Intelligence Through Advancements in Onboard AI Processing: A review
authors: Domenico Barretta, Luca Marini, Francesca Razzano, Giorgia Guerrisi, Sanna Sandberg, Roberto del Prete, Valentina Zancan, Eleonora Mariotti, Gianluca Furano, Livia Manovi, Jian Guo, Silvia Liberata Ullo, Nicolas Longépé, Gabriele Meoni
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Magazine
abstract: The Earth Observation (EO) sector is rapidly evolving into Earth Action (EA), with onboard Artificial Intelligence (AI) processing emerging as a key enabler. This technology offers strategic advantages through 1) enabling autonomous and low-latency EO missions with adaptive data processing capabilities that overcome the limitations of ground-based post-processing in handling the vast data volumes produced by growing satellite constellations and 2) supporting the evolution toward AI-driven and distributed EO mission architectures. This review explores the key technological advancements, mission architectures, and emerging paradigms that are shaping the next-generation EO systems. Pioneering EO missions are presented to showcase current capabilities, while the commercial, technical, and operational implications are analyzed alongside key challenges and ongoing research efforts (January 2020 to March 2025) aimed at enabling Real-Time (RT) Earth system intelligence.

title: First Cooperative Formaldehyde Monitoring with Chinese Morning and Afternoon Satellites: Revealing Global Multi-Temporal Concentration Dynamics
authors: Hongri Hou, Wenjing Su, Haoran Liu, Chengxin Zhang, Cheng Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Formaldehyde is an important trace gas in the radical cycle of the troposphere with significant impacts on human health. Conducting global observations of hazardous trace gases at multiple times daily is essential to identify the evolutionary patterns as well as the factors influencing their concentrations. At present, such ability mainly relies on geostationary satellites, which are limited in observing certain regions. Here, we present a research approach that employs near-Earth orbit nadir-viewing satellites (DQ1, GF5B) equipped with the same instrument (EMI-02) to perform two observations on formaldehyde on a daily basis between December 1, 2022 and November 30, 2023. We develop a synergic retrieval process to jointly utilize data from both satellites. The satellite observations were validated against multiple MAX-DOAS stations，yielding relatively strong correlations (R=0.679-0.849 for GF5B, R=0.458-0.911 for DQ1) and consistent seasonal trends between morning and afternoon data. Validation against Pandora sites also shows good correlations (R=0.569-0.938 for GF5B, R=0.523-0.884 for DQ1). We then examine three representative regions to examine the diurnal variation of tropospheric formaldehyde. We also conducted cross-validation against GEMS and S5P. And we further investigated the trend differences across several representative regions. This analysis further enables us to categorize different areas based on the dominant factors influencing formaldehyde formation. This methodology can be further extended to other satellite series and additional trace gases.

title: R2aft: A Speedy and Highly Available RDMA-based Consensus Protocol
authors: Zhiyuan Dong, Haitao Song, Zhaoguo Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Parallel and Distributed Systems
abstract: Consensus protocols are essential in distributed systems for fault tolerance, significantly impacting the performance and availability of the upper systems. Recent works have leveraged Remote Direct Memory Access (RDMA) for its superior performance and one-sided primitives to accelerate consensus protocols. Beyond performance, RDMA's processor bypass feature allows servers to handle network requests even when processors fail, enabling a fine-grained failure model where servers remain operational despite processor issues. This fine-grained failure model can help enhance the availability of existing consensus protocols, which traditionally assume processor failures lead to complete server crashes. In this paper, we introduce R2aft (RDMA+Raft), the first RDMA-based consensus protocol fully adapted to this model. Beyond the availability benefits, R2aft also integrates specific optimizations to capitalize on RDMA's high performance. Using the YCSB Workload, we show that R2aft incurs only a 12.2% latency overhead while maintaining throughput comparable to Mu, the state-of-the-art RDMA-based consensus protocol.

title: Fast Algorithm for Optimal Resource Allocation in a Low-Latency Downlink NOMA-Enabled Vehicle Platoon-Based V2X System
authors: Dony Darmawan Putra, Ka-Cheong Leung, Wan-Jen Huang
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication enables timely exchange of safety and control information among vehicles, roadside units, and the broader network, and is therefore central to realizing stable, high-density platooning under real-world conditions. In this work, we study low-latency downlink resource allocation for non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA)-enabled vehicular platoons while accounting for realistic mobility and sensing imperfections. Unlike rate- or throughput-centric approaches, latency is treated as the primary objective, which is optimized subject to per-user quality of service (QoS) constraints as well as both global and per-platoon power budgets. The main contributions are as follows: (i) explicit incorporation of dynamic vehicle velocities and an imperfect-CSI model so that allocations adapt to mobility-induced channel fluctuations and estimated delays; (ii) a latency-minimization formulation that integrates the minimum-rate and power bounds per platoon together with the dual-level power constraints to reflect the practical infrastructure and platoon limitations; (iii) an analytical convex reformulation that provides provable optimality and enables predictable real-time operation; and (iv) closed-form intra-platoon power allocations derived from Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT) conditions that involve the Lambert W function, yielding interpretable per-vehicle rules and explicit minimum-platoon power expressions for admission control. Numerical results indicate that the proposed NOMA scheme yields dramatic latency improvements compared with other recent benchmarks, while the individual NOMA rates comfortably exceed the minimum threshold. Moreover, the average latency grows only modestly with increasing numbers of users and platoons, and raising the minimum-rate target is shown to further reduce latency. The proposed bisection-based algorithm converges rapidly (error typically below 10−6 in about 10 iterations) and cuts down floating-point operations (FLOPs) by a factor on the order of the number of users and number of platoons when compared with other recent methods.

title: Decouple Re-identification and Human Parsing for Occluded Person Re-Identification
authors: Shuguang Dou, Yuanpeng Tu, Yanna Lin, Junyao Gao, Cairong Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biometrics, Behavior, and Identity Science
abstract: This paper proposes a Decouple Re-identificatiOn and human Parsing (DROP) method to learn the task-specific features that fit the two tasks for occluded person re-identification (ReID). Previous multi-task learning approaches for occluded ReID typically either (1) jointly learn person ReID and human parsing from global features, or (2) leverage semantic information from parsing to guide attention, with the latter generally achieving superior performance. The paper attributes this discrepancy to the differing granularity requirements of ReID and human parsing features. To address this, we propose DROP, a framework that explicitly decouples ReID and parsing features. Specifically, we firstly introduce a Hierarchical Bias-Decoupled Adapter (HBDA) to extract task-specific representations for human parsing. To further separate the two tasks, human positional information is incorporated only into the parsing branch with a pedestrian position encoder to provide semantic spatial context, while the ReID branch employs a Part-aware Compactness Triplet (PCT) loss to enhance part-level discriminability at the instance level. Experimental results underscore the efficacy of DROP compared to the two prevailing mainstream methods, especially the Rank-1, which reached 77.2% on Occluded-Duke. The codebase of DROP will be available.

title: A 91.4-dB SNDR 2-MS/s 18-bit SAR ADC With Enhanced Optimal-Combination-Algorithm-Based Calibration
authors: Yanjin Lyu, Haoran Li, Haotian Wei, José M. de la Rosa, Yuanqi Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: This work presents a 2-MS/s 18-bit successive-approximation-register analog-to-digital converter (ADC). Acapacitor digital-to-analog converter (CDAC) and a resistor digital-to-analog converter (RDAC) are cascaded to avoid the linearity limitation caused by parasitic capacitors on the bridge capacitor between the CDAC and the RDAC. An optimal-combination-algorithm (OCA)-based calibration is adopted to improve the linearity of the CDAC and the matching between CDAC and the bridge capacitor, and a least significant bit (LSB)-side dynamic element matching (DEM) strategy is applied to further improve the CDAC linearity. As for the RDAC, this work makes it calibration free by combining the parallel R-unit and R-2R topology. Measurement results show that the proposed ADC can achieve 91.4-dB signal-to-noise and distortion ratio (SNDR), 12.0-parts-per-million (ppm) (3.1-LSB) integral non-linearity (INL), and 174.0-dB FOM<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">S,SNDR</sub> (including the power consumed by the calibration logic), and the worst-case INL across 25 dies improves from 72.5ppm (19.0LSB) to 18.8ppm (4.9LSB) after the OCA-based calibration and theLSB-side DEM. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, this is the first high-precision (resolution <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\geq 16$</tex-math> </inline-formula> bit) data converter adopting OCA-based calibration.

title: TriFusNet: A Triple-Fusion Convolutional Network for Multivariate Time Series Classification_supp1-3679706.pdf
authors: Wenhan Liu
abstract: Multivariate time series classification (MTSC) plays a critical role in a wide range of real-world applications, such as healthcare, finance, and industrial monitoring. This paper proposes a triple-fusion network (TriFusNet), a novel convolutional network designed to address the challenges of MTSC. TriFusNet employs a specialized architecture that captures both variable-specific features and features shared across variables through the parallel use of standard, depth-wise, and shared-kernel convolutions. A hierarchical triple-fusion strategy is introduced to enhance representation learning across three stages: input-level fusion transforms raw variables, intermediate-level fusion integrates heterogeneous features, and output-level fusion improves decision robustness. Extensive experiments on 26 benchmark datasets show that TriFusNet outperforms 15 competitive baselines, achieving the best average rank (4.3077) with a Win/Draw/Loss of 5/4/17. The effectiveness of its architectural design and parameter settings is empirically validated, and a qualitative theoretical discussion is conducted to support the proposed fusion strategy. These results highlight TriFusNet's strong potential for real-world applications involving complex and high-dimensional time series data.

title: Random Jungle: A Nature-Inspired Improvement of Tree-Based Classifiers
authors: Insaf Kraidia, Mohammad Mominur Rahman, Ashhadul Islam, Samir Brahim Belhaouari
abstract: Tree-based ensemble methods remain central to structured and tabular classification tasks; however, classical Random Forest and boosting approaches exhibit limitations in modeling complex feature interactions, handling noisy or imbalanced data, and balancing robustness with computational cost. This paper proposes Random Jungle (RJ), a heterogeneous ensemble framework that enhances structural diversity through operator-driven feature transformations while preserving the interpretability of tree-based models. RJ integrates three key components: (i) Label DensityWeight (LDW) for refined probability estimation under class imbalance and noise, (ii) heterogeneous operator forests based on additive, subtractive, multiplicative, and power transformations to capture complementary feature interactions, and (iii) adaptive aggregation via weighted integration to balance predictive stability and representation diversity. Unlike boosting methods that rely on sequential error correction, RJ increases structural expressiveness through parallel operator diversification, offering a principled compute–accuracy trade-off. Extensive evaluation on 19 benchmark datasets against 9 widely used classifiers demonstrates consistent top-ranked performance across multiple metrics. RJ achieved near-perfect accuracy on Glass Identification (99.12%), Breast CancerWisconsin (98.88%), and Balance Scale (96.76%), and obtained the best overall average ranking (1.28), outperforming Decision Tree (2.71) and Random Forest/XGBoost (3.28). Runtime and memory analyses further quantify the dimensional expansion and computational footprint, enabling practical assessment for real-world deployment. These results establish RJ as a robust, interpretable, and structurally diversified ensemble framework for high-dimensional classification tasks.

title: Non-monotonic causal discovery with Kolmogorov-Arnold Fuzzy Cognitive Maps
authors: Jose L. Salmeron
abstract: Fuzzy Cognitive Maps constitute a neuro-symbolic paradigm for modeling complex dynamic systems, widely adopted for their inherent interpretability and recurrent inference capabilities. However, the standard FCM formulation, characterized by scalar synaptic weights and monotonic activation functions, is fundamentally constrained in modeling non-monotonic causal dependencies, thereby limiting its efficacy in systems governed by saturation effects or periodic dynamics. To overcome this topological restriction, this research proposes the Kolmogorov Arnold Fuzzy Cognitive Map (KA-FCM), a novel architecture that redefines the causal transmission mechanism. Drawing upon the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem, static scalar weights are replaced with learnable, univariate B-spline functions located on the model edges. This fundamental modification shifts the non-linearity from the nodes' aggregation phase directly to the causal influence phase. This modification allows for the modeling of arbitrary, non-monotonic causal relationships without increasing the graph density or introducing hidden layers. The proposed architecture is validated against both baselines (standard FCM trained with Particle Swarm Optimization) and universal black-box approximators (Multi-Layer Perceptron) across three distinct domains: non-monotonic inference (Yerkes-Dodson law), symbolic regression, and chaotic time-series forecasting. Experimental results demonstrate that KA-FCMs significantly outperform conventional architectures and achieve competitive accuracy relative to MLPs, while preserving graph based interpretability and enabling the explicit extraction of mathematical laws from the learned edges.

title: CCDM: Channel-Aware Contrastive Conditional Diffusion for Multivariate Probabilistic Forecasting
authors: Siyang Li, Yize Chen, Hui Xiong
abstract: High-quality time series probabilistic forecasting is greatly demanded by decision makers to tackle many engineering and societal problems under uncertainties. Modern forecasting methods center around employing conditional time series diffusion models to capture the target temporal predictive distribution. Although diffusion-based forecasting methods have gained great traction, the precision and reliability of their produced prediction intervals are still unsatisfactory due to the inadequate predictive likelihood learning. Besides, it remains an open issue to effectively represent the complex multivariate temporal correlations across the sequential denoising process. To address them, we propose a channel-aware <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">C</u>ontrastive <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">C</u>onditional <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">D</u>iffusion <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">M</u>odel termed CCDM to achieve desirable multivariate probabilistic forecasting. Specifically, we first devise an ad-hoc denoising-based temporal contrastive learning method to enhance the conditional likelihood learning efficiency for the real predictive distribution. This contrastive training can align with vanilla step-wise noise regression manner and amplify the useful predictive information extracted from past sequences. Then, we develop a channel-aware denoising diffusion transformer to recognize both intra-channel and crosschannel temporal correlations in the heterogeneous multivariate time series at each diffusion step. Lastly, we validate the proposed CCDM can showcase stronger accuracy, scalability and generalizability versus existing excellent generative forecasting methods, with average 31.42% and 30.80% increases on deterministic and probabilistic forecasting capacity. Our code can be accessible at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/LSY-Cython/CCDM</uri>.

title: Spectral-Spatial Enhanced Local Contrast Strategy for Hyperspectral Small Air Target Detection_supp1-3678377.pdf
authors: Lianru Gao
abstract: Detecting small air target is an important task in civil aviation. However, the weak characteristics of these targets make detection challenging. Hyperspectral image (HSI), provides a new approach for the small air target detection task due to its strong ability of capturing both spatial and spectral information simultaneously. In this article, we propose a spectral-spatial enhanced local contrast strategy for hyperspectral small air target detection. An unsupervised band selection step based on the local contrast strategy has been designed based on local contrast (LC-UBSM) to choose bands with better distinguish ability between the target and background in HSI. Then, we have developed an improved RX detection algorithm with combined spatial and spectral variance (CSSV-RX) to detect the target while suppressing both background and noise. Experimental results on both real GAOFEN-5 dataset and simulated dataset based on EO-1 (Earth Observing-1) satellite have validated the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method.

title: Efficacy of High-Fidelity VR Threat-and-Error Simulation for Competency-based Pilot Training
authors: Teong Leong Chuah, Brian Soon Wei Chiam, Ahmad Iqbal bin Othman, Lindy Li Wen Lim, Jia Wang Tay, Vinh-Thuyen Nguyen-Truong, Catherine Wan Ting Leo, Leslie Siew Mun Chong, Jussi Keppo, Eng Tat Khoo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: The aviation industry faces increasing pilot training demands, and reliance on conventional Full Flight Simulators (FFS) limits training capacity. Virtual Reality (VR) offers scalable, remote training opportunities, but its role as a complement to FFS requires empirical validation. In collaboration with Singapore Airlines (SIA) instructor pilots, we developed a VR training prototype for a visual approach into Gimhae International Airport, emphasizing Competency-Based Training and Assessment (CBTA)-based Threat and Error Management (TEM). An empirical study with 39 SIA Boeing 737-MAX 8 type-rated first officers evaluated VR against FFS. An equivalence analysis showed that VR achieved performance outcomes comparable to FFS in 13 of the 16 Observable Behaviors (OBs) and across 4 Competencies. In addition, a comparative analysis indicated measurable performance improvements when VR was used to supplement FFS training. Our results suggest VR can meaningfully complement FFS in targeted competency areas, with future work required to assess its broader integration across additional scenarios and performance metrics.

title: Which Strategy When? Designing an Adaptive Search System for Virtual Reality
authors: Jalal Safari Bazargani, Fatema Rahimi, Abolghasem Sadeghi-Niaraki, Soo-Mi Choi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Searching for information, objects, or places in virtual reality (VR) is often a cumbersome process that breaks user immersion. Existing interaction techniques like pointing or voice commands are context-dependent, yet current systems fail to help users select the optimal strategy for a given situation. To address this challenge, we introduce an adaptive VR search framework that recommends search strategies based on situational factors. We first propose a taxonomy of five common VR search strategies and construct a factorial dataset linking task types, object properties, and environmental conditions to strategy choices. We then conduct a two-stage study. In Stage 1, we capture users’ natural strategy selections in a free-search environment to create a ground-truth dataset. In Stage 2, we use this data to train and evaluate two adaptive systems, one driven by a machine learning (ML) model and another by a Large Language Model (LLM), against the free-search baseline. Our results show that both adaptive systems significantly reduce the number of attempts and perceived workload compared to free search. While the ML-based system achieved the fastest task completion times, both adaptive approaches were rated as significantly more usable. This work demonstrates that adaptive, context-aware systems can enhance search efficiency and user experience in VR, paving the way for more intelligent and immersive information-seeking interfaces.

title:
Quantum-Well Oxide Semiconductor Transistors Overcome Mobility–V
                    <sub>T</sub>
                    Trade-Off
authors: Jimin Kang, Koustav Jana, Jack C. Evans, Nathaniel Safron, Donglai Zhong, H.-S. Philip Wong, Shengman Li
abstract: We present a quantum-well oxide semiconductor (OS) channel to overcome the mobility-threshold voltage (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula> <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\text {FE}} -\text {V}_{\text {T}}\text {)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> trade-off in OS field-effect transistors (OSFETs). For the same channel thickness (t<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\text {CH}}\text {)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, the quantum-well OSFET outperforms among different channel configurations, delivering the highest <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula> <sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">FE</sub>, optimum V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">T</sub>, and the sharpest turn-on behavior, as indicated by a narrower transition region (V<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\text {TR}}\text {)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>. Capacitance-voltage measurements, together with a physics-based analytical model, confirm carrier confinement of quantum-well and link the strategy through barrier-height (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Phi _{\text {B}}\text {)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> and barrier-thickness (t<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\text {B}}\text {)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> tuning. Density functional theory (DFT) analysis identifies quantum-well channel candidates and establishes their applicability across diverse material systems; we then validate the approach in WO<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\text {X}}/$</tex-math> </inline-formula>In<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{{3}}/$</tex-math> </inline-formula>WO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">X</sub> and doping-profile engineered n<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}^ + -$</tex-math> </inline-formula> In<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{{3}}/$</tex-math> </inline-formula>n − In<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{{3}}/$</tex-math> </inline-formula>n<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}^ + -$</tex-math> </inline-formula> In<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> channels. In addition, the quantum well channel exhibits minimal V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">T</sub> roll-off of 36mV down to 60nm channel length, and strong positive bias stress reliability with only 10mV of V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">T</sub> shift at 2MV cm<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}^{-{1}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> for 1000s. Finally, a dual-gated In<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{{3}}/$</tex-math> </inline-formula>SnO<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{{2}}/$</tex-math> </inline-formula>In<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> quantum-well OSFET achieves <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula> <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\text {FE}} =33.8$</tex-math> </inline-formula> cm<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}^{{2}}/$</tex-math> </inline-formula>Vs, V<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\text {T}} =0.15$</tex-math> </inline-formula> V, and V<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\text {TR}} =0.47$</tex-math> </inline-formula> V.

title: Computational Modeling of Aptamer Targeting Triple-Negative Breast Cancer Cells
authors: Anvi Joshi, Gaurav Sharma
abstract: Breast cancer is a global health challenge and one of the most common cancers in women, with 2.3 million cases and 685,000 deaths reported in 2020. Beyond health impacts, it significantly affects quality of life and psychological well-being. EpCAM, a transmembrane glycoprotein overexpressed on breast cancer cells, promotes tumor growth and metastasis. Aptamers, single-stranded oligonucleotides or peptides that fold into 3D structures, bind specific molecular targets with high specificity via van der Waals, hydrogen bonding, and electrostatic interactions. We hypothesize that aptamers bind to the EpCAM receptor site breast cancer cells. AlphaFold 3 predicted the EpCAM receptor structure. Aptamer modeling with UNAFold and FARFAR2 allowed the design of sequences with precise binding capabilities. Binding energy calculations assessed interaction strength and PLIP analysis identified specific protein-ligand interactions, including hydrogen bonds and hydrophobic contacts. The aptamer with the most favorable binding energy and robust interactions demonstrated strong potential for targeted applications. This aptamer selectively binds EpCAM. This targeted strategy enhances therapeutic precision and efficacy while minimizing side effects. This study highlights the potential of EpCAM-targeting aptamers in breast cancer therapy. Future research using in vitro and in vivo models could validate these findings and advance aptamer conjugates for clinical use, offering a promising approach for precision-targeted breast cancer treatment.

title: A Novel AI System for Generating and Optimizing Interior Designs for Personalized Spaces
authors: Atharva Abhyankar
abstract: Interior designing spaces is time consuming, requires specialized tools, and skilled professionals. This is cost prohibitive for most smaller scale projects. This research project aims at democratizing the interior design process and making it accessible to all, including professionals.

title: Cybersecurity Assessment of Smart Grid Exposure Using a Machine Learning Based Approach
authors: Mofe O. Jeje
abstract: Given that disturbances to the stable and normal operation of power systems have grown phenomenally, particularly in terms of unauthorized access to confidential and critical data, injection of malicious software, and exploitation of security vulnerabilities in a poorly patched software among others; then developing, as a countermeasure, an assessment solutions with machine learning capabilities to match up in real-time, with the growth and fast pace of these cyber attacks, is not only critical to the security, reliability and safe operation of power system, but also germane to guaranteeing advanced monitoring and efficient threat detection. Using the Mississippi State University and Oak Ridge National Laboratory dataset, the study used an XGB Classifier modeling approach in machine learning to diagnose and assess power system disturbances, in terms of Attack Events, Natural Events and No-Events. As test results show, the model, in all the three sub-datasets, generally demonstrates good performance on all metrics, as it relates to accurately identifying and classifying all the three power system events.

title: A Caenorhabditis Elegans Model Challenges Meclizine Potential as a Lifespan-Extending mTORC1 Inhibitor
authors: Aradhya Vijay
abstract: Aging research aims to uncover interventions that can extend lifespan and improve healthspan. Meclizine, an antihistamine and mTORC1 inhibitor, has shown potential as a longevity-promoting agent in mammalian studies, but its effects on other organisms are unclear. This study investigates whether meclizine can extend the lifespan of Caenorhabditis elegans (C. elegans), a well-established model organism in aging research. C. elegans at the L4 larval stage were divided into two groups: one treated with 3 uM meclizine and a control group treated with 0.25% DMSO. Survival was monitored daily, and lifespan data were analyzed using the Log Rank (Mantel-Cox) and Gehan-Breslow-Wilcoxon tests. Statistical analysis revealed no significant difference in survival between the meclizine-treated and control groups (p = 0.1920, Log Rank; p = 0.0834, Gehan-Breslow). Hazard ratios and median survival comparisons similarly showed no significant impact of meclizine on C. elegans lifespan. The study concludes that meclizine does not significantly extend the lifespan of C. elegans under the conditions tested. These findings suggest that meclizine’s potential as a lifespan-extending compound may be species-specific or dose-dependent, however, further investigation is needed to fully elucidate its effects.

title: Students’ Sustainable Career Building Strategy using Intelligence Learning – An Innovative Approach to Preserve National Intellectuals
authors: Sai Kasturi Kamila, Sai Namam Kamila
abstract: Dropout rates in college and high school are dramatically increasing. A recent study from Think Impact [2] reported that on average more than 32% undergraduate students have already dropped out in 2025. Further, it also reports 59% of students either could not complete or took more than 4 years to complete their graduation program. As students slip into this dropout crisis, they get entrapped in an impoverished unescapable cycle ultimately leading to long-term negative social and economic consequences. Educators understand the need for AI incorporation in the educational system; nevertheless, no true implementation of AI has been done to predict helpful options. A previous study [3] with the initial concept of building a career predicting AI model by monitoring students’ academic, extracurricular, social, and behavioral interest was presented revealing that such a model is truly possible. Thus, this study explores this AI engine and creates an architectural model integrating numerous variables and parameters (extracurriculars, school data, etc.) using generated data points to develop a career predicting machine learning model. The working model solely outlines that this classification is certainly possible with binary diagnostics. This model has the potential to revolutionize the nation by offering personalized career options that not only categorize students into tailored pathways but also emphasize their unique strengths and aspiration. By leveraging these insights, education systems can cultivate and nurture their nation’s intellectual capital, ensuring strategic preservation, growth, and development of a highly skilled and clear-minded workforce for the future.

title: Teaching AI to Non-STEM Students: A General Education Approach Using Space Weather and Low-Code Tools
authors: James Liporace, Katherine G. Herbert, Vaibhav K. Anu, Rebecca A. Goldstein, Sumi Hagiwara, Stefan Robila, Thomas J. Marlowe
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is an increasingly important topic across many disciplines, impacting industries and social institutions beyond traditional STEM fields. As AI becomes a part of daily life, it is crucial to introduce AI concepts to non-STEM students. This paper discusses an interdisciplinary approach to teaching AI at Rockland Community College, a two-year institution within the State University of New York (SUNY) system. Our curriculum integrates AI literacy into general education requirements, allowing students to fulfill the Natural Science General Education requirement. A central focus is the application of AI in analyzing solar weather patterns using low and no-code tools. We explore pedagogical strategies, course design, and student engagement techniques to make AI accessible and applicable across various fields.

title: Comments and Corrections Corrections to “YOLOv8-SCS: Improved Object Detection for Autonomous Driving Under Adverse Weather Conditions”
authors: Younggyu Lee, Jinho Kang
abstract: Presents corrections to the paper, (YOLOv8-SCS: Improved Object Detection for Autonomous Driving Under Adverse Weather Conditions).

title: A GAMLSS Framework for Crop Discrimination Using Geodesic Polarimetric SAR Parameters
authors: Willams B. F. da Silva, Avik Bhattacharya, Abraão D. C. Nascimento, Alejandro C. Frery
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: In remote sensing, Polarimetric Synthetic Aperture Radar (PolSAR) data provide rich information for land cover characterization, particularly for vegetation monitoring. In this study, we investigate the statistical behavior of two roll-invariant geodesic parameters, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha \_{\text{GD}}$</tex-math></inline-formula> and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathrm{P}\_{\text{GD}}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, derived from PolSAR data for crop characterization. These parameters are computed from geodesic distances in the space of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$4 \times 4$</tex-math></inline-formula> real matrices. We analyze their temporal evolution across different crop types and growth stages. Using the Generalized Additive Model for Location, Scale, and Shape (GAMLSS) framework, we model the relationships among <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha \_{\text{GD}}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathrm{P}\_{\text{GD}}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, crop type, and growth stage, and propose a classifier based on their joint stochastic information. Results from a comparative study with three existing methods show modest overall accuracies; however, the proposed approach consistently performs better in scenarios with less distinct class boundaries. Our results demonstrate that the GAMLSS approach effectively distinguishes between different crop types and their growth stages. Furthermore, the combined use of both parameters yields stronger discriminative power than using either parameter alone, thereby improving the characterization of crop types and phenological stages.

title: A Hybrid Voltage Level Modulation for Dual Transformer LCL Immittance Network Resonant Dual-Active-Bridge Converter
authors: Rui Wang, Xiaodong Li, Shi-Yuan Liu, Song Hu, Hao Chen
abstract: This article proposes a hybrid voltage level modulation (HVLM) strategy for a dual-transformer inductor–capacitor–inductor (LCL) immittance network resonant dual-active-bridge (DT-LCL INR DAB) converter to simultaneously optimize conduction and switching losses. The proposed HVLM strategy combines two modes to eliminate backflow power, reduce root-mean-square (rms) current, and achieve zero-voltage switching (ZVS). In the range of medium to full-load, the full half-bridge mode eliminates backflow power on both sides and reduces the rms current. Under light-load conditions, the half-bridge mode still achieves zero backflow power on the secondary side. In addition, the proposed scheme enables full-range ZVS operation for all switches. Compared with dual-phase-shift and enhanced dual-phase-shift strategies applied on a single-transformer LCL INR converter, the proposed method further reduces rms current, particularly under light-load. The effectiveness of the proposed method is validated through a 400 W experimental prototype.

title: Terahertz Integrated Sensing and Communication: Sensing Comes Before and For Communication
authors: Chong Han, Yejian Lyu, Ziming Yu, Guangjian Wang, Cheng-Xiang Wang
abstract: The Terahertz (THz) band offers vast spectrum resources that enable ultra-high-rate communication and high-precision sensing, positioning THz integrated sensing and communication (THz-ISAC) as a key technology for future wireless systems. This paper provides a sensing-centric investigation of THz-ISAC, reviewing representative THz sensing applications and clarifying why sensing naturally precedes and supports THz communication. Two complementary THz measurement platforms are introduced, from which sensing-channel data and a large-scale material database are obtained. Building on these datasets, the paper summarizes core signal-processing techniques for THz sensing, including high-resolution parameter estimation, environment reconstruction, and material identification. Leveraging empirical measurements, a hybrid THz sensing channel model is developed, and environment reconstruction results are demonstrated, achieving millimeter-level accuracy. Finally, open challenges and future research directions are discussed.

title: Journal of Microelectromechanical Systems Publication Information
authors: N/A

title: Dynamic Event-triggered H ∞ Control for Networked Control Systems with Stochastic Delay: An Envelope-Guided Partial Reset Approach
authors: Hongwei Yang, Jiaoyang Wang, Hanmei Zhou, Lanfeng Hua, Jin Yang, Kaibo Shi, Qishui Zhong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: The event-triggered H∞ control problem is addressed for networked control systems subject to stochastic transmission delay. A novel dynamic event-triggered mechanism (DETM) integrating an envelope-constrained dynamic variable and a time-varying threshold is proposed to manage measurement data packet releases. The DETM employs an adaptive convex-combination partial-reset strategy, ensuring strict boundedness of the internal dynamic variable while achieving co-optimization of triggered thresholds and controller parameters. Initially, the network channel delay is modeled as a distributed delay with a probability density function kernel, leading to a closed-loop control system formulation within the distributed-delay framework. Subsequently, through construction of the Lyapunov-Krasovskii functional (LKF) that incorporates both the bounded dynamic variable and the delay distribution kernel, sufficient conditions are derived to guarantee asymptotic stability with prescribed H∞ performance, enabling the development of an effective co-design algorithm. Finally, comparative simulation results demonstrate that the proposed DETM significantly reduces communication load while maintaining the desired performance and exhibits strong robustness to stochastic delay.

title: Hierarchical Gradient Preserved Network for High Resolution Hyperspectral Fusion Imaging
authors: Changda Xing, Meiling Wang, Yongchang Xu, Cheng Wang
abstract: High resolution hyperspectral fusion imaging (HRHFI) is an important topic in the remote sensing related tasks, which integrates advantages of hyperspectral images (HSIs) and multipectral images (MSIs) to generate the high resolution HSIs (HR-HSIs). Traditional methods fail to maintain multi-scale change information and directional information of HSIs and MSIs during the HRHFI process, limiting the performance. To solve this challenge, a novel hierarchical gradient preserved network (HGPN) is proposed for HRHFI. Concretely, we begin by establishing the foundational observation model, which serves to make an initial estimate of HR-HSI so that the basic observation loss is thus constructed. Subsequently, we integrate both multi-scale spectral gradient preservation loss and multi-scale spatial gradient preservation loss into the basic observation loss, which results in the overall loss function of the proposed HGPN method. Further, an optimization strategy based on alternating directions is devised to solve the overall loss and derive the corresponding solutions, which finalizes the imaging fusion mapping. Different from traditional approaches, the proposed HGPN method can achieve hierarchical fusion, which retains multi-scale gradient information of input HSI/MSI for HRHFI.
