Extensive experiments have been implemented to verify that the proposed HGPN method achieves more competitive performance than several state-of-the-art approaches in the HRHFI task.

title: EgoPoseVR: Spatiotemporal Multi-Modal Reasoning for Egocentric Full-Body Pose in Virtual Reality
authors: Haojie Cheng, Shaun Jing Heng Ong, Shaoyu Cai, Aiden Tat Yang Koh, Fuxi Ouyang, EngTat Khoo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Immersive virtual reality (VR) applications demand accurate, temporally coherent full-body pose tracking. Recent head mounted camera-based approaches show promise in egocentric pose estimation, but encounter challenges when applied to VR head-mounted displays (HMDs), including temporal instability, inaccurate lower-body estimation, and the lack of real-time inference. To address these limitations, we present EgoPoseVR, an end-to-end framework for accurate egocentric full-body pose estimation in VR that integrates headset motion cues with egocentric RGB-D observations through a dual-modality fusion pipeline. A spatiotemporal encoder extracts frame- and joint-level representations, which are fused via cross-attention to fully exploit complementary motion cues across modalities. A kinematic optimization module then imposes constraints from HMD signals, enhancing the accuracy and stability of pose estimation. To facilitate training and evaluation, we introduce a large-scale synthetic dataset of over 1.8 million temporally aligned HMD and RGB-D frames across diverse VR scenarios. Experimental results show that EgoPoseVR outperforms state-of-the-art egocentric pose estimation models. A user study in real-world scenes further shows that EgoPoseVR achieved significantly higher subjective ratings in accuracy, stability, embodiment, and intention for future use compared to baseline methods. These results show that EgoPoseVR enables robust full-body pose tracking, offering a practical solution for accurate VR embodiment without requiring additional body-worn sensors or room-scale tracking systems.

title: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics Publication Information
authors: N/A

title: A Novel Frequency Stabilization Method for External Cavity Diode Lasers Based on PZT Modulation
authors: Rui Xu, Fangjun Qin, Zhichao Ding, An Li
abstract: The method of frequency stabilization using injection current modulation can cause mode hopping in the laser. It also fails to fully utilize the benefits of PZT modulation for narrowing the laser linewidth. This study addresses the issue of laser frequency drift in external cavity semiconductor lasers, which is caused by fluctuations in temperature, injection current, and PZT voltage, adversely affecting measurement accuracy. To mitigate these problems, we propose a frequency stabilization method based on PZT modulation. By designing a PZT modulation demodulation device and optimizing the modulation parameters, we achieve a high stability frequency locking for the laser, with long-term stability reaching 1.12352 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-9</sup>@1000s. Experimental results demonstrate that our method is not only structurally simple and cost-effective, but it also satisfies the frequency requirements for various lasers, particularly those that have lower stability demands, such as the repumping laser and probe laser used in the field of quantum precision measurement.

title: RAMPSNet: A Rotation-Aware Multi-Granularity Polarimetric Spatial Network for PolSAR Image Classification
authors: Lei Wang, Liangliang Han, Rong Gui, Yu Shi, Ying Zhu, Ma Lei
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Polarimetric Synthetic Aperture Radar (PolSAR) image classification is a fundamental remote sensing interpretation, which remains challenging because polarimetric responses vary strongly with target orientation and radar incidence angles. These variations introduce complex backscattering properties that are difficult to model. To address this issue, we present the Rotation-Aware Multi-Granularity Polarimetric Spatial Network (RAMPSNet), which adopts a hierarchical and modular design to extract rotation-consistent features from both the PolSAR data rotation domain and multi-granularity polarimetric spatial perspective. RAMPSNet consists of three core modules. Specifically, the Rotation-Aware Module (RAM) explicitly models dependencies along the rotation dimension by combining a Learnable Rotation Positional Embedding (LRPE), a Rotation Aware Self-Attention (RASA) mechanism, and a lightweight feed-forward network. These components jointly capture global and local rotational relationships and allow RAM to extract stable and rotation-consistent polarimetric features. The Multi Granularity Feature Extraction Block (MGFEB) splits the input along the channel dimension into three parallel branches: a global frequency-aware branch, a local multi-kernel convolution branch, and an identity mapping branch. This design enables MGFEB to extract complementary structural, textural, and fine-grained polarimetric spatial features for richer semantic information learning with low computational overhead. Finally, the Channel Spatial Interaction Module (CSIM) sequentially models channel and spatial dependencies to enhance discriminative features, sup press redundancy, and ensure structural coherence and produces discriminative final representations. Experiments on three widely used PolSAR datasets show that RAMPSNet consistently achieves higher accuracy than state-of-the-art methods, as well as fewer parameters and lower computational cost.

title: Realistic Wavelength-Dependent Modeling of Ultra-Wideband Semiconductor Optical Amplifiers
authors: Hartmut Hafermann, Xiaohui Zhao, Flavio Nogueira Sampaio, Yann Frignac
abstract: We present a physics- and measurement-informed, device-agnostic lumped reservoir model of the wavelength-dependent gain compression of semiconductor optical amplifiers. Its parameters can be extracted from a few measurements of the gain characteristic, one wavelength at a time. The model is shown to be in good agreement with experimental measurements and reproduces the predictions of the Connelly model, at a fraction of the computational cost. The model is further generalized to describe time dependent nonlinear effects, and it is demonstrated that accounting for their wavelength dependence can reduce dynamic nonlinear penalties. Implementation of the dynamic model on graphics-processing units provides acceleration by up to three orders of magnitude, rendering the model much faster than typical fiber simulations for the same problem size. For even faster estimation of nonlinear penalties, the Agrawal model is generalized to account for the wavelength- dependence of the gain. We describe an algorithm to obtain effective working-point-dependent parameters for the Agrawal model by locally matching the measured gain characteristic at the operating point. This yields a proxy for the dynamic model that enables a fast closed-form estimate for the wavelength-dependent nonlinear penalty in good agreement with simulation.

title: IEEE Microwave Theory and Techniques Society Information
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Terahertz Science and Technology

title: Leader-Follower Cooperative Transportation of a Circular Object by Swarm Robots Based on Local Information
authors: Yuto Fukao, Takahiro Endo, Fumitoshi Matsuno
abstract: This paper presents a leader-follower control method in which swarm robots, consisting of multiple autonomous and distributed units, cooperate to transport a circular object. Unlike previous approaches that require all robots to possess global information about the object and the goal or to communicate with one another, our method requires only a single leader robot to possess global knowledge and uses no inter-robot communication. Each follower robot operates in a fully distributed manner, autonomously determining its motion based solely on local information obtained within its sensing range. The followers contribute to the transportation task by pushing the object toward the leader while preserving the connectivity of the group. As a result, even robots with limited capabilities can collectively perform the transportation task based on local information. The method is structured into five phases. A mathematical analysis is provided to demonstrate that each phase achieves its intended purpose, ensuring that the object’s velocity vector successfully aligns with the direction toward the leader. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is validated through both dynamic simulations and real-world robotic experiments, which confirm that the object is successfully moved toward the leader.

title: An Overview of Polarization, Frequency and Beam Reconfigurable Antennas
authors: Filipa Antunes, Amélia Ramos, Tiago Varum, João N. Matos
abstract: Reconfigurable antennas dynamically alter intrinsic radiation parameters such as polarization, operating frequency, and beam steering. Their relevance has rapidly increased in recent years to support multiple satellite constellations and to mitigate interference in high data-density wireless environments, including emerging 5th Generation and 6th Generation networks. In this context, this paper surveys the state-of-the-art in reconfigurable microstrip patch antennas through a novel structure-based classification that organizes designs by where reconfiguration occurs within the antenna (feeding network, radiating element, substrate, ground plane, meta-antenna). Moreover, this document systematically compares antenna designs across three categories: Mono-Reconfigurable (single parameter), Hybrid Reconfigurable (two independent parameters), and Multi-Reconfigurable (three independent parameters), revealing which technique combinations enable multi-parameter designs and which remain unexplored. Additionally, the antennas mentioned in each architecture are analyzed regarding their respective enabling technologies (e.g., Positive-Intrinsic-Negative diodes, varactor diodes), as well as their design approaches with detailed comparison tables providing quantitative performance metrics. Finally, dominant trends and current limitations are identified and discussed, particularly the finding that over 60% of all designs operate below X-band, highlighting critical research gaps for 5th Generation and 6th Generation applications requiring K- and Ka-band operation. The advantages, trade-offs and implementation challenges of the different design techniques are discussed, and research directions with the greatest potential for advancing next-generation reconfigurable adaptive antenna systems, are outlined.

title: PERCEL: A Re-Writable NVM CIM Incorporating a CTT-Based Per-Cell DAC
authors: Samyak Chakrabarty, Vinod Jacob, Mohammadreza Zeinali, Georgios Karfakis, Siyun Qiao, Ziyi Guo, Puneet Gupta, Subramanian Iyer, Sudhakar Pamarti
publish: IEEE Journal on Exploratory Solid-State Computational Devices and Circuits
abstract: Compute in memory (CiM) accelerators perform matrix vector multiplications (MVMs) directly inside memory arrays, reducing data movement and improving both energy efficiency and throughput for AI workloads. To reduce the number of conversions, recent designs use multi-bit compute cells. Nevertheless, practical multi-bit CiM still faces a tension between accuracy, efficiency, and re-writeability, since multi-level NVM based designs suffer from nonlinearity and poor re-writeability, while multi-level activation based DRAM / SRAM macros are limited by mismatch and low accuracy. This work introduces a per-cell DAC based CiM macro that combines the density of multi-level NVM with fully re-writable DRAM weights to break the trade-off. Each bit cell embeds a compact 6-bit CTT based current mode DAC, calibrated in-situ through a write verify write loop, together with a 1T(1C) embedded DRAM. Post-layout simulations of a 576×256 macro in 22nm FDSOI project 49.9 8b-TOPS/W, and 8.96 8b-TOPS/mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, while system level evaluation on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 with ResNet-50 shows less than 0.2% accuracy loss relative to a digital baseline, without the need for any Hardware-aware Training.

title: RMMNet: Deep Memory Aided Extended Object Tracking via High-Order Markovian Modeling and State Decoupling
authors: Zhixing Wang, Le Zheng, Shi Yan, Ruud J. G. van Sloun, Nir Shlezinger, Yonina C. Eldar
abstract: Grounded in Bayesian filtering (BF), random matrix-based extended object tracking (EOT) offers an efficient framework for the joint estimation of a target’s kinematic state and extension. However, the performance of these methods is fundamentally constrained by the first-order Markov assumption, which often fails to capture the high-order Markovian dynamics prevalent in real-world scenarios. To overcome this limitation, we introduce RMMNet, a novel EOT method that integrates a deep memoryaided recursive neural network filter within a BF framework, enabling robust tracking of targets that exhibit high-order Markovian dynamics. Initially, we propose an approximate model under a high-order Markovian assumption and derive the implementation of its BF framework. Thereafter, Gaussian approximation and moment matching are employed to derive the analytical formulations for the proposed BF framework. Finally, based on the closed-form formulations, we design the architecture of RMMNet as an end-to-end trainable recursive neural networkbased filter. Experimental results on both simulated and real-world data demonstrate that our method outperforms traditional EOT approaches and other state-of-the-art deep learning methods.

title: Learning Robot Visual Navigation in Crowds via Intention-Aware Scene Representations_supp1-3677748.pdf
authors: Jiankun Wang
abstract: Robot crowd navigation requires the ability to infer human intentions while accounting for the structural constraints of the environment. Currently, deep reinforcement learning (DRL) provides a promising method for learning navigation policies that understand human intentions. However, most of them rely on limited scene representations, treating pedestrians as simple 2D points and ignoring rich visual cues from both humans and the environment. To address this issue, iCrowdNav, a novel visual crowd navigation method with intention-aware scene representations, is introduced to encode behavioral and structural context from egocentric visual observations. Our method employs two key components: a spatio-temporal encoder for extracting occupancy features of the scene, and Intent-Interact Former (I2Former), an attention-based module that encodes human poses to infer pedestrians' motion intentions. These features are integrated into a compact state embedding that supports effective DRL policy training. Extensive experiments show that our method achieves superior performance over baselines, and real world deployment demonstrates vision-based crowd navigation.

title: Multi-Similarity Preserving Semantic-Aware Hashing for Cross-Modal Retrieval_supp1-3680156.pdf
authors: Hongmei Chen
abstract: Hashing-based methods have gained widespread attention in cross-modal retrieval due to their significant potential for spatiotemporal efficiency and query accuracy, driving significant progress. However, most existing algorithms extract similarity information solely from labels, overlooking the consistent semantic similarities embedded in features. Additionally, some algorithms underutilize label semantics, neglecting inherent category relationships and their interplay with feature semantics, which compromises algorithmic effectiveness. Furthermore, current methods often suffer from weak discriminative power and semantic misalignment when embedding similarity information into hash codes, leading to learning biases. To address these short-comings, a Multi-similarity Preserving Semantic-aware Hashing (MPSH) algorithm is proposed. MPSH first learns a shared semantic space from heterogeneous features to generate dynamic feature fusion similarity. Subsequently, it explores label distributions to construct category vectors, which are collaboratively associated with isomorphic feature representations to form a semantic-aware space. Finally, manifold embedding is employed to effectively preserve multi-similarity and data distribution, yielding semantically rich hash representations. Moreover, a non-relaxed discrete optimization algorithm is introduced to accelerate hash code iteration. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets, comparing MPSH with leading algorithms, demonstrate its superior efficiency and performance.

title: SCOTT-Gait: A Spiking Cross-attention Temporal Triple-loss Network for Occlusion-compensated Gait Recognition
authors: Ying Tao, Shengyu Gao, Yue Zheng, Chip-Hong Chang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biometrics, Behavior, and Identity Science
abstract: Occlusions significantly degrade gait recognition performance, particularly when only anonymized representations like binary silhouettes are provided instead of original videos due to privacy concerns. Existing methods either rely on computationally expensive silhouette reconstruction or extract occlusion-invariant features, which often underperform under severe occlusions. To address these limitations, we propose SCOTT-Gait – a Spiking Neural Network (SNN) that integrates spatial and temporal occlusion compensation mechanisms directly into the recognition model for accurate gait recognition under occlusions. Spatial compensation is achieved through a deformable spiking-based convolution, which infers missing structural information by modeling the co-movement between visible and occluded regions within a binary silhouette image. These adaptively sampled, geometrically correlated samples are processed by the SNN backbone augmented with a Temporal Cross-Attention (TCA) module, which is designed to address temporal compensation by enhancing the network’s ability to aggregate temporal features. A Cycle-Consistency Triplet Loss (CCTL) is introduced to further align features across the walking period to ensure that similar poses are grouped closer together in the feature space, even if they are from different points of the walking cycle. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world occluded gait datasets demonstrate that our method maintains competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art CNN-based approaches, while remaining computationally efficient - a remarkable milestone as the first vision-based SNN gait recognition with occlusion. In particular, it has the most robust performance when trained on naturally, mildly occluded samples and evaluated on severely occluded synthetic samples.

title: One-bit Single-Measurement Spectral Compressed Sensing via Hankel Matrix Factorization_supp1-3680334.pdf
authors: Jinsheng Li
abstract: This paper studies the problem of recovering a spectrally sparse signal from its one-bit single-measurement vector. Most previous methods depend on multiple measurement vectors, which face inherent challenges in real-time critical scenarios. Regarding the single-measurement vector, only a few on-grid approaches have been proposed; however, these techniques suffer from estimation errors due to grid mismatch. To facilitate gridless reconstruction under a one-bit single-measurement vector, a novel optimization model is devised by leveraging both the low-rank property of the lifting Hankel matrix and the sign consistency between the target signal and its estimate. Consequently, a onebit spectral compressed sensing algorithm via Hankel matrix factorization, termed OSCAR, is proposed by incorporating a one-sided ℓp norm smoothing technique. Furthermore, an accelerated OSCAR algorithm, named Acc-OSCAR, is developed by integrating momentum techniques to expedite convergence. Theoretical results provide the convergence analysis for the proposed algorithms. Numerical results confirm the excellent performance of the proposed algorithms.

title: A Machine Learning Approach for Drug Discovery from Plant-derived Natural Products: Prediction of Biological Activities of Secondary Metabolites and Enzyme Commission Numbers of Enzymes
authors: Leoni Kim
abstract: Natural Products (NP): Substance found in nature including secondary metabolites

title: The Rise of DAOs in Gaming: Governance, Economy, and Career Shifts
authors: Veer Varma, Aaliya Varma
abstract: Problem: Traditional game development sidelines players, reducing engagement and innovation. Solution: A DAO-

title: A Multi-Level Group Characterization and Sparse Interaction Network for Pedestrian Trajectory Prediction
authors: Yunheng Xu, Jing Liu, Rui Xu, Jie Chen, Yingsong Li
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Accurate pedestrian trajectory prediction is crucial for autonomous systems, yet remains challenging due to the complexity of social interactions, particularly within groups. Existing methods often overlook the nuanced influence of velocity on group behavior or inefficiently model interactions, this paper proposes a novel group-aware graph network that integrates multi-level group features such as position and velocity and a sparse interaction module. Our key innovations include a learnable grouping mechanism and efficient feature fusion strategy. We conducted a series of experiments on the walking pedestrians dataset (ETH), crowds dataset (UCY), Stanford Drone Dataset (SDD) and ApolloScape dataset. On the SDD dataset, the average displacement error (ADE) and final displacement error (FDE) of the model reached 0.31/0.51; on the ETH/UCY dataset, the average ADE and average FDE of the model reached 0.24/0.41; on the ApolloScape dataset, the model achieves optimal trajectory prediction performance in the pedestrian class, with ADE/FDE of 0.58/0.83, respectively. Ablation studies have shown that our group feature inscriptions and sparse interaction modules have respectively reduced the average ADE by 15% and 8%, and constructed a deployable, lightweight framework.

title: A New Method for Hydrophobicity Evaluation Based on Tangential Transfer: Beyond Yes-or-No, Towards Discrimination
authors: Bo Tao, Li Cheng, Bin Cao, Wenlong Xu, Shuo Zhang, Ruijin Liao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Room temperature vulcanized (RTV) silicone rubber coatings have been widely applied in power grids owing to their excellent hydrophobicity and hydrophobicity transfer. Among these, hydrophobicity transfer is a key property of outdoor insulation materials, enabling insulators to maintain high resistance to pollution flashover under long-term contamination. IEC 62039 recommends two methods for evaluating hydrophobicity transfer; however, their discriminative capability is limited and the acceptance criteria are relatively loose. As a result, some coatings perform well during initial installation but exhibit a rapid decline in hydrophobicity transfer during service. To address this limitation, a new quantitative evaluation method based on tangential transfer is proposed and validated experimentally on six RTV coatings. The results show that, when the inter-group coefficient of variation is used as the discriminative index, the method provides more than eightfold higher discrimination than the standard methods. It significantly amplifies the performance differences among coatings and enables effective ranking. Furthermore, in combination with the adsorption–cohesion theory, this study explores the correlation between the evaluation results and material properties. TG-DSC, swelling test, Soxhlet extraction, and GC–MS analysis are employed to determine the coating formulation, organic phase crosslink density, and the content and species of low molecular weight (LMW) siloxanes. The results reveal a strong linear correlation between the outcomes of the new method and the concentration of D4–D5. This finding is highly consistent with the transfer theory of LMW siloxanes, thereby confirming the scientific validity of the method. In summary, the proposed method provides a high-discrimination quantitative assessment of the intrinsic hydrophobicity transfer capability of RTV coatings and offers a scientific basis for material selection in engineering practice.

title: A Comparative Analysis of Deep Learning Architectures for Satellite Image Classification: Integrating Performance, Uncertainty, and Optimization Geometry
authors: Paritosh Dwivedi, C. Deepthi Chowdary, Aditya Hiremath, Sairabanu J
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Land use and land cover (LULC) classification from satellite imagery requires models that are accurate, reliable, and interpretable under operational conditions. We present a unified comparative study of five deep learning configurations on EuroSAT—27,000 Sentinel-2 images across 10 classes from 34 European countries—evaluated on 2,700 held-out test samples. Architectures include LeNet-5, ResNet-18, and a Vision Transformer (ViT), tested under RGB-only and four-channel RGB plus Near-Infrared (RGB+NIR) inputs. Adding NIR raises ResNet-18 accuracy from 83.30% to 91.52%. A five-member Deep Ensemble of independently trained ResNet-18 (RGB+NIR) convolutional neural networks (CNNs) achieves 95.07% ± 2.32% (Wilson 95% CI: 94.19%–95.82%), at approximately four times the training cost of a single model. Beyond accuracy, we analyze predictive uncertainty via ensemble variance and loss-landscape sharpness via the Hessian trace (Hutchinson’s estimator). The ensemble’s gain arises from solution diversity, not individual flatness. Our contribution is a methodological framework for holistic evaluation—not state-of-the-art (SOTA) competition.

title: MSSW-Based Simultaneous Characterization of Low-Damping Ferrite Thin Films: Thickness, M s , and Δ H
authors: Zequn Zeng, Si-Ping Gao, Yongxin Guo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: Film thickness, saturation magnetization <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$M_{\text {s}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, and FMR linewidth <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Delta H$</tex-math> </inline-formula> are three key material parameters of a low-damping ferrite thin film. Conventionally, they are characterized separately using different measurement setups according to different mechanisms. In this article, a magnetostatic surface spin wave (MSSW)-based characterization method is proposed for simultaneously measuring the three parameters. Since MSSW propagation is strongly dependent on the three parameters, they can be extracted from the MSSW measurement at the same time. The proposed method requires only one-time vector network analyzer (VNA) measurement of the low-damping ferrite sample on top of an MSSW transmission line (MSSW-TL) test fixture, and it enables rapid characterization at lower RF power than conventional methods. Two yttrium iron garnet (YIG) samples with different thicknesses were used to validate the proposed method. Good agreement between the measured and vendor data was observed, proving the accuracy, convenience, and practical applicability of the proposed method.

title: Tailoring the Matteucci Effect in amorphous glass-coated microwires by thermal annealing
authors: M. Kozachok, L. Fečová, K. Richter
abstract: The Matteucci effect, or induction of the electrical voltage in ferromagnetic wire upon application of torsion, provides a straightforward method for converting magnetic fields into electrical currents. Unlike in the classical experiment, however, amorphous glass-coated microwires can exhibit a Matteucci-induced voltage even without applied torsion. Two underlying mechanisms of voltage induction—domain wall propagation and domain wall (de)pinning - are engineered to enhance the resulting voltage. It is demonstrated that appropriate thermal treatment of microwires can increase the induced voltage by more than a factor of three.

title: PoD-BNG for Mega-Metropolitan Networks: Reliable and Scalable Broadband Network Gateway Evolution
authors: Zaifeng Lin, Xia Gong, Yongqing Zhu, Bing Yang, Ke Ruan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: Given the wide range of growing online commerce, reliable broadband access has become essential to connecting households and business. In the past twenty years, several solutions have been developed for this purpose, from BNG to vBNG and to BNG-CUPS most recently. However, existing solutions suffer from constrained scalability, inefficient hardware utilization, and an absence of robust fault tolerance. Consequently, these deficiencies have significantly hindered the deployment of carrier-grade access networks in densely populated areas. In response to this, we proposed Pooled Disaggregated Broad-band Gateway (PoD-BNG), an architecture featuring a shared user plane resource pool and an integrated warm standby topology for seamless failover. We validate its performance using a probabilistic model parameterized with real-world operational data. Our analysis shows that a “3+1” standby topology offers the optimal balance of cost and resilience, increasing hardware utilization by 45% and reducing operational and energy costs by 70% compared to legacy BNGs. Furthermore, the architecture is fundamentally fault-resistant, with the probability of a catastrophic outage being less than one in a billion. The success of PoD-BNG is validated by its large-scale deployment since 2020, serving over 46 million households by the end of 2024, establishing it as a superior solution for next-generation access networks.

title: LLM-hRIC: LLM-Empowered Hierarchical RAN Intelligent Control for O-RAN
authors: Lingyan Bao, Sinwoong Yun, Jemin Lee, Tony Q.S. Quek
abstract: Despite recent advances in applying large language models (LLMs) and machine learning (ML) techniques to open radio access network (O-RAN), critical challenges remain, such as insufficient cooperation between radio access network (RAN) intelligent controllers (RICs), high computational demands hindering real-time decisions, and the lack of domain-specific fine-tuning. Therefore, this article introduces the LLM-empowered hierarchical RIC (LLM-hRIC) framework to improve the collaboration between RICs in O-RAN. The LLM-empowered non-RT RIC acts as a guider, offering a strategic guidance to the nearreal- time RIC (near-RT RIC) using global network information. The RL-empowered near-RT RIC acts as an implementer, combining this guidance with local real-time data to make near-RT decisions. We evaluate the feasibility and performance of the LLM-hRIC framework in an integrated access and backhaul (IAB) network setting, and finally, discuss the open challenges of the LLM-hRIC framework for O-RAN.

title: Modeling and Control for UAV With Off-Center Slung Load
authors: Zongyang Lv, Yanmei Jia, Yongqing Liu, Alan F. Lynch, Qing Zhao, Yuhu Wu
abstract: Uncrewed aerial vehicle (UAV) with slung load system is a classic air transportation system. In practical applications, the suspension point of the slung load does not always align with the center of mass (CoM) of the UAV due to mission requirements or mechanical interference. This offset creates coupling in the system’s nonlinear dynamics which leads to a complicated motion control problem. In existing research, modeling of the system are performed about the UAV’s CoM. In this work we use the point of suspension instead. Based on the new model, a cascade control strategy is developed. In the middle-loop controller, the acceleration of the suspension point is used to regulate the swing angle of the slung load without the need for considering the coupling between the slung load and the UAV. An inner-loop controller is designed to track the UAV’s attitude without having to simplify the coupling effects. We prove local exponential stability of the closed-loop using Lyapunov function analysis. Finally, simulations and experiments are conducted to validate the proposed control.

title: Predictable Latency-Aware Traffic Allocation for Seamless Wi-Fi Roaming in TSN Industrial IoT
authors: Zhechen Liu, Bin Wu, Haojun Yang, Xiaoqin Wang, Yu Tang, Peng Cheng
abstract: With the increasing adoption of embodied intelligence and autonomous mobile systems in industrial environments, automated guided vehicles (AGVs) impose stringent requirements on latency performance and communication reliability in time-sensitive networking (TSN)-related scenarios. However, under continuous mobility and frequent handovers, conventional single-link roaming mechanisms and latency-agnostic multi-link transmission schemes do not explicitly provide mechanisms to jointly support low latency, low jitter, and reliable connectivity. This limitation stems not only from the lack of effective models for deriving achievable medium access delay under heterogeneous access point (AP) load and contention conditions, but also from the absence of joint AP selection and traffic allocation mechanisms driven by such predictions. To address these challenges, this paper proposes Latency-Aware Traffic Allocation (LATA), a latency-aware multi-link seamless roaming and traffic allocation framework designed to operate within existing IEEE 802.11 mechanisms, without introducing protocol modifications or requiring specialized hardware extensions. For mixed nonsaturated and near-saturated network conditions, a Medium Access Control (MAC)-layer delay analysis model is developed to estimate the MAC-layer service delay—defined as the sum ofMAC queueing delay andMAC access delay—for an individual station associated with a given AP, leveraging observable channel utilization statistics and locally available transmission history. Based on the predicted delay, an adaptive AP selection and traffic allocation strategy is further developed, allowing dynamic switching among single-link transmission, load balancing, and packet-level redundancy during roaming. Simulation results based on ns-3 demonstrate that, under the evaluated scenarios, LATA reduces MAC-layer service delay compared with conventional single-link schemes and fixed-redundancy multi-link solutions while maintaining communication reliability, indicating its potential applicability in TSN-oriented scenarios where the MAC-layer service delay behavior influences end-to-end performance.

title: DDQN-Based Uplink Resource Allocation in LEO Satellite-Enabled IoT Networks
authors: Donghyeon Kim, Haejoon Jung, In-Ho Lee
abstract: This paper delves into a resource allocation strategy for uplink multibeam low-Earth orbit (LEO) satellite communication systems, with the objective of maximizing the system sum rate while satisfying the minimum data-rate requirements of individual users. In particular, we address a subchannel allocation problem, which remains challenging due to high outage probabilities and the substantial computational complexity associated with conventional optimization-based methods. Although deep Q-network (DQN)-based approaches have been widely investigated to alleviate these challenges, their achievable performance remains limited. To overcome these shortcomings, we propose a distributed deep Q-network (DDQN)-based subchannel allocation scheme that enhances the effectiveness of existing deep learning techniques. Simulation results confirm that the proposed approach significantly improves both sum-rate and outage performance, while considerably reducing computational overhead compared with conventional DQN-based methods.

title: The Gap Between Computational Thinking and Coding, and How to Bridge it
authors: Claire Lee, Jiyoon Park, Catherine Shin
abstract: Learning a foreign language usually involves mastering words and grammar to understand sentence patterns. In contrast, coding languages have fewer keywords and simpler grammar, yet they allow for diverse solutions through combinations. This complexity often leads to uncertainty about which syntax to apply in specific situations during problem-solving, making coding challenging. Computational Thinking (CT) aims to bridge this gap by suggesting when to use certain syntax, but Wing's CT concept focuses on human problem-solving, not aligning directly with coding. Meanwhile, Scratch's CT approach is effective for teaching basic computational concepts but lacks a robust theoretical framework for problem solving.This paper proposes a hybrid method combining these strengths: using block coding like Scratch for foundational learning, and applying Wing’s CT from a computer perspective for problem-solving. It showcases a developed website aimed at implementing this educational strategy. The site (https://block4py.org) is publicly available and aims to assist beginners in coding.

title: An IoT-Based Warning System for Children inside Hot or Stolen Cars
authors: Rhianna Fang
abstract: Almost every year, there have been reports about children dying from heatstroke inside hot cars because they were left or became trapped in a car. Based on the statistical data from the website of Kids and Car Safety [1], since 1990, the United States alone has a total of 1,125 child deaths which is about 33 child deaths annually. Another 7,500 children have survived in extremely hot cars but with varying levels of injury. Despite the warnings of "Once You Park, Stop. Look. Lock." from National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) [2] and social media for years, there were still 40 fatalities reported in 2024, which remains a critical safety concern for families with children and vehicles.Besides this, another similar but extremely important issue is cars being robbed with children still remaining inside. Within the recent decade, the United States has about 1,753 cars stolen with a child inside [3]. The tragic child death numbers in hot cars, plus additional similar incident numbers of car theft involving children, emphasizes the urgent need for solutions.The traditional car security camera will be shut down after turning off the car engine or electric vehicle motors. This poster presents an Internet of Things (IoT) based early warning system designed to prevent such two kinds of tragedies from the very beginning. The proposed system utilizes a network of sensors to monitor the weight change of child car seats or boosters, temperature, carbon dioxide levels, and security cameras to detect any sensitive motion inside vehicles as well. When potentially dangerous conditions are detected, the system will trigger an immediate alert to parents, any caregivers, nearby adults, and even emergency services. The proposed IoT-based system will be developed in two steps with many key features included.The first step is to develop a safety warning system for children in hot cars. Some weight sensors can be installed underneath child seats or boosters to detect weight change. The weight sensor could be switched on if its detected weight increases more than a certain amount like 3 pounds. Within a few seconds once the driver stops the car and leaves the driver’s seat, if the child seat weight sensor is still on, the empty driver seat can trigger a signal sent to the drivers via a smart cellphone to remind the driver to look at the back seat for children. The always-working weight sensors could be charged by the driving car or a small solar panel installed on the car dashboard. One critical issue with the first step is reliability such as if your mobile phone loses the network signal. The second step of this warning system is to develop an IoT-connected multiple devices including real-time weight and thermal sensors, carbon dioxide monitoring, car security cameras and alarms system. The geofencing feature and multi-channel alert system could be added to track vehicle location for stolen cars, make audible alarms, and notify your mobile phones or even the emergency services.

title: A Vision System to Detect 3D Wound Surfaces for Plasma Medical Applications
authors: Vyaas Baskar, Bhavya Bellannagari, Sohail Zaidi
abstract: The use of robots in surgical processes and hospitals has become more common throughout the last decade. These robots can perform complex operations with a generally low risk to patients. This study describes a vision system that detects a wound and calculates the position of key points on the wound surface in 3D. This vision system was developed for use with a triple-jointed robotic arm that traces the wound surface, applying plasma to accelerate the wound healing process. First, computer vision methods, implemented with OpenCV in Python, detect the wound in real time. Checks are performed to ensure that the detected contours truly match those of the wound. Then, the coordinates of key points on the wound are calculated in 3D space, relative to the origin of the robotic arm. These can be sent to the processor controlling the robotic arm, which would then control it to follow a path between each of the key points. The developed vision system was found to detect the wound and calculate the positions of the key points with high accuracy. This paper will describe wound detection in more detail and the calculations used to determine the position of the key points.

title: Combating Free-Riding in AIGC Service System: a Decentralized Reputation-based Model Management Approach
authors: Jiajia Jiang, Moting Su, Fengshu Li, Xiangli Xiao, Yushu Zhang
abstract: Blockchain strengthens copyright protection for AI generated content (AIGC) by establishing a transparent and traceable management framework, which encourages model providers to participate in forming an AIGC service system, collectively driving the development of AIGC and offering high-quality, reliable content generation services to a broader audience. Despite blockchain enabling full traceability in AIGC generation, some malicious model providers may exploit free riding actions by intercepting user requests and forwarding them to other providers to save computational resources or pursue greater profits, and then returning the generated product to users directly or with slight modifications. Such behavior hinders users from accessing high-quality AIGC service resources and threatens the legitimate rights of honest model providers, which may ultimately diminish their enthusiasm to participate in the AIGC service system, disrupting the balance of value co-creation within the system. To combat such free-riding behaviors and ensure equitable benefit distribution among participants, we propose a decentralized reputation-based model management approach within the blockchain-enabled AIGC service system, reducing the probability of malicious service providers par ticipating while providing users with a reliable reference for model selection. Moreover, it protects the content generation through a timestamp-based watermark to prevent malicious alteration and unauthorized use, safeguarding the interests of all participants during the generation process and enhancing the reliability and security of the AIGC service system. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach can effectively constrain and supervise model behaviors, successfully combating free-riding actions in the AIGC service system, and providing a reliable and intuitive reference for users in model selection.

title: Simultaneous Feature and Instance Selection via Evolutionary Auxiliary Optimization_supp1-3677086.pdf
authors: Ruwang Jiao
abstract: In the big data era, the large volume and high dimensionality of data challenge many data mining algorithms. Since many classification algorithms are sensitive to data distribution, removing redundant or noisy features and instances can greatly improve their performance. Feature selection and instance selection are two major data reduction techniques that are inherently interconnected. This article proposes an auxiliary-optimization-assisted constrained multiobjective optimization method that concurrently tackles feature and instance selection, with two key constraints: forcing the obtained subsets to have better classification performance than that of using all features and instances under the given classification algorithm, and bounding the worst-class error. A simple but effective initialization method is designed to sample initial solutions relatively uniformly across different regions of the objective space, to provide a diverse and high-quality set of initial feature and instance subsets. An auxiliary-optimization-based search approach is proposed to fully utilize useful infeasible solutions, which can further reduce the number of selected features and instances without compromising classification performance. The proposed method is compared with a number of promising methods on 20 real-world classification datasets, and the experimental results show that it is generally better than those methods. Additionally, the proposed method offers a significant advantage whereby the majority of its solutions exhibit superior or comparable classification performance compared to using all original features and instances, while selecting no more than 30% of the features and 50% of the training instances across most datasets.

title: Editorial
authors: Heinz-Wilhelm Hübers, Martina Wiedner, Vincent Desmaris
publish: IEEE Transactions on Terahertz Science and Technology

title: Path Tracking Method for Intelligent Vehicles Based on Adaptive Model Predictive Control with Tire Stiffness Estimation
authors: Xingyi Liu, Xianyong Lv, Haochun Yang, Jianhua Guo, Yinhang Wang
abstract: Path tracking is one of the core tasks for intelligent vehicles. Under continuously varying driving conditions, tire forces exhibit strong nonlinear characteristics. Model Predictive Control (MPC) based on a linear tire model may suffer from significant prediction errors because it does not account for the nonlinear variations of longitudinal stiffness and cornering stiffness, thereby making it difficult to ensure high-accuracy trajectory tracking. To address this issue, this paper proposes an Adaptive Model Predictive Control (AMPC) approach that explicitly considers tire-force calculation errors. First, an Adaptive Sliding Mode Observer (ASMO) is employed to estimate the longitudinal tire force. Second, by integrating the High-order Cubature Kalman Filter (HCKF) with the Strong Tracking Filter (STF) concept, a Singular Value Decomposition-based High-order Strong Tracking Cubature Kalman Filter (SVD-HSTCKF) is developed to estimate the lateral tire force. Then, correction factors for longitudinal and lateral stiffness are calculated based on the deviation between the estimated tire forces and those predicted by a linear tire model, and these factors are used to dynamically update the prediction model. Finally, the proposed AMPC strategy is validated through simulation. Results show that, compared with conventional MPC and LQR methods, the AMPC approach reduces lateral displacement errors by 47% and 69%, and yaw angle errors by 45% and 72%, respectively, significantly enhancing path tracking accuracy and control robustness.

title: A Two-Stage Spatiotemporal Trajectory Optimization Framework for Autonomous Lane Changing With Dynamic Risk Fields
authors: Zhihao Lin, Zhen Tian, Xianxian Zhao, Hanyang Zhuang, Ming Yang, Jianglin Lan
abstract: Autonomous vehicles (AVs) face challenges in making accurate decisions and planning optimal trajectories in complex environments. Current methods often overlook future dynamics or are too computationally demanding for real-world use, with many studies limited to simple scenarios. To address these limitations, this paper introduces a two-stage trajectory planning framework that considers safety, efficiency, and ride comfort simultaneously in dynamic environments. The key contribution is a novel Dynamic Risk Field (DRF) that provides a unified representation of static and dynamic obstacles, enabling comprehensive risk assessment without treating them separately. The first stage generates spatially feasible trajectories using quintic polynomials in the Frenet frame, guided by the DRF to assess risks from obstacles. The second stage optimizes these trajectories temporally with Space-Time (ST) graphs and Sequential Quadratic Programming (SQP), ensuring collision avoidance and smooth dynamics. Compared to existing methods, our framework achieves efficient real-time planning through the two-stage decomposition while maintaining trajectory quality. Simulations across highway lane changes, overtaking, and intersection scenarios demonstrate that the framework produces safe, efficient, and comfortable trajectories, outperforming benchmark algorithms in terms of acceleration and jerk metrics.

title: SCHMITT: A Novel Data Acquisition System for Nuclear Reactor Physics Experiments
authors: Gilles Caulier, Maxime Dutertre, Georges Gay, Romain Boffy
abstract: The SCHMITT Data Acquisition System (DAQ) is a new, modular platform designed for nuclear reactor physics experiments, replacing the legacy X-MODE system. Developed by the IRESNE institute (CEA, Cadarache), SCHMITT focuses on acquiring digital TTL signals, enabling precise measurements of reactor kinetic parameters such as prompt decay constant and effective delayed neutron fraction. The hardware, based on off-the-shelf equipment (CAEN R7771 pulse train recorder), supports up to 3 Mc/s with 25 ns resolution, while the custom software—built on Qt, C++, and CMake—offers real-time data processing, visualization, and advanced algorithms (e.g., Power Spectral Density, Rossi-α, and reactivity computation). SCHMITT was validated in laboratory tests and successfully deployed during a 20 GW power transient in the CABRI reactor, demonstrating its robustness and accuracy. This system enhances experimental flexibility, data management, and remote operation, making it a valuable tool for nuclear research and reactor safety studies.

title: Integrated Distribution-Free Detection and Adaptive Robust Scheduling for V2G Smart Home Microgrids
authors: Boyu Wang, Zhihong Li, Wan Li, Zhiyuan Wang
abstract: Residential smart homes equipped with photovoltaic (PV) systems, battery storage, and vehicle-to-grid (V2G) enabled electric vehicles (EVs) depend on a home energy management system (HEMS) to schedule power flows. False data injection (FDI) attacks that subtly corrupt PV or load measurements can increase electricity costs and battery degradation. These attacks often evade conventional alarms. Existing approaches often separate anomaly detection from scheduling or require labeled attacks and Gaussian error models. This can result in missed attacks, costly false alarms, or overly conservative operation. This work introduces a unified framework, the Solar-Focused Theoretical Hybrid Wasserstein-Adaptive (SF-THWA) model, which integrates distribution-free attack detection with adaptive robust scheduling. Both forecast errors and FDI appear as distribution shifts in net demand. SF-THWA (i) derives time-varying robustness parameters for Wasserstein distributionally robust optimization (DRO) scheduling and (ii) detects attacks using a majority-vote ensemble. Experiments using real residential data show that integrating detection and scheduling reduces conditional value-at-risk (CVaR) by 20% compared to scheduling alone. In contrast, bolt-on detection with standard DRO increases costs by 27%, underscoring the need for integration. The hybrid ensemble achieves F1 = 0.934 at 2.5σ with zero false alarms. The breaking-point margin at 2.25σ establishes a quantifiable security envelope. All methods solve within 60 seconds on consumer-grade hardware.

title: Is Seeing Believing and Hearing Trusting? Exploring the Role of Visual and Vocal Cues in Shaping User Trust in VR News Anchors
authors: Yi GAO, Zixuan Guo, Hai-Ning Liang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Amid the global decline in news credibility, immersive journalism in virtual reality (VR) offers opportunities to enhance trust and engagement through presence and interactivity. As the communicators of news, virtual news anchors play a key role in shaping audience perceptions. However, the mechanisms through which virtual anchors in VR elicit audience trust remain underexplored. Guided by the Stimulus-Organism-Response framework, this study investigates how the visual style and voiceover of VR news avatars influence perceived realism, and how these perceptions subsequently shape trust and news credibility. In a 2×2 within-subjects experiment (N = 24), participants viewed a virtual news anchor featuring either (1) a realistic or (2) a cartoon-like visual style, paired with either (1) an anthropomorphic or (2) a neutral voiceover. Results indicate that visual style exerted a dominant influence on perceived realism and trust in virtual anchors. By contrast, while voiceover type did not affect perceived realism, perceived voice realism significantly predicted trust, highlighting the nuanced role of vocal cues in VR news contexts. Notably, the interaction between visual and vocal realism failed to yield an additive effect on trust, and avatar trust did not significantly predict news credibility. Our findings shed further light on avatar trust in immersive news environments and provide design insights for the development of credible and trustworthy virtual anchors

title: MuscleGolfAR: Embodied versus Detached Visualization for Motions and Inferred Muscle Activations in Augmented Reality
authors: Ruofan Liu, Chen-Chieh Liao, Takuya Takahashi, Yichen Peng, Erwin Wu, Hideki Koike
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Precise kinematic and dynamic representations are equally critical for fine-grained motor skills such as golf, yet the latter remains underexplored. This work introduces MuscleGolfAR, an augmented reality (AR) training system integrating swing motions and muscle activities. To support cost-effective inference of muscle activations, we construct a multimodal dataset encompassing posture, electromyography (EMG), and plantar pressure. The system displays inferred EMG through embodied (first-person perspective) and detached (third-person perspective) visualizations. User studies are subsequently conducted to evaluate the impact of these strategies on training effectiveness. Results reveal that embodied visualizations enhance ownership over augmented feedback, while detached visualizations facilitate a holistic comprehension of the whole body. Furthermore, individuals' preferences for these strategies correlate with their practice habits and skill proficiencies, offering new insights for the design of AR-based motor skill training systems.

title: An Integrated Electrochemical Biosensor Array Enabling Extracellular Vesicles Disruption and Multiplexed Lung Cancer Biomarker Detection_supp1-3680013.pdf
authors: Palash Kumar Basu
abstract: We introduce the EV-Disrupt and Detect System (EDDS), an innovative, portable biosensor utilizing electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) for the swift, low-volume, and economical assessment of extracellular vesicle (EVs)-related lung cancer biomarkers. The EDDS combines electric field-induced EVs disruption with the simultaneous detection of 4 critical biomarkers: TSG101, EGFR, GPC1, and GM2AP, directly from serum. EVs disruption occurred within 30 seconds utilizing a 50 mV, 1 kHz square wave, with disruption efficiency validated by nanoparticle tracking analysis (93.9%) and Western blotting to ensure protein integrity. Post-disruption, the released cargo was quantified by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) across 4 specialized screen-printed electrodes (SPEs), with results corroborated by enzyme-linked immunosorbent test (ELISA). The electric field parameters (voltage, frequency, and duration) were optimized with 150 μL of serum, yielding a 0.218–2.809-fold enhancement in detectable biomarker concentrations. The EDDS markedly decreases processing time, cost, and technological complexity by obviating the necessity for traditional EVs isolation techniques such as ultracentrifugation or chromatography. This integrated platform facilitates direct EVs disruption and multiplexed biomarker identification within a singular workflow, providing a robust instrument for minimally invasive cancer diagnostics and advancing broader clinical applications in liquid biopsy.

title: BlockPred-DRL: A DRL Reward Design Method Based on CNN Blocking Prediction for RMSA in EON
authors: Lingye Yang, Lei Wang, Hanyun Luo
abstract: Existing reward designs for DRL-based Routing, Modulation, and Spectrum Assignment (RMSA) in Elastic Optical Networks (EONs) often emphasize immediate allocation outcomes while neglecting the risk of future blocking. To address this limitation, we propose a DRL reward design that incorporates Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)-based blocking prediction. Sequences of network spectrum states are transformed into multi-channel two-dimensional ‘‘spectrum distribution maps’’ and preprocessed with two topological features: the ‘‘Link-Level Spectrum Occupancy Ratio’’ and the ‘‘Adjacent-Link Co-Occupancy Ratio.’’ The CNN analyzes spatial and short-term temporal patterns in spectrum distribution maps to predict blocking probabilities, which are integrated into the DRL reward function. This approach guides the agent’s policy toward long-term network stability. Additionally, we introduce a dynamic path weight method that increases the weights of paths with failed allocations, enhancing their selection probability during action selection. This reduces reliance on fixed paths and promotes path reactivation. Simulations on the NSFNET topology show that our method reduces the blocking rate by 43.64% relative to K-Shortest Path First-Fit (KSP-FF), 32.61% relative to DeepRMSA, and 20.51% relative to both DRL-Cut and DRL-Fragsize. In addition, at 250 Erlang on NSFNET, steady-state results indicate a 2.25% reduction in spectrum fragmentation compared with the existing DRL method that achieves the lowest fragmentation. Overall, these results highlight our approach’s effectiveness in improving spectrum utilization efficiency and network performance in EONs.

title: The Duality of “Carry” and “Find” in Mobile and Pervasive Systems
authors: Mahadev Satyanarayanan
abstract: This article examines when a mobile system should view a resource as portable (and hence carried by its user), and when it can be viewed as pervasive (and hence easily found whenever and wherever needed). It shows that the anticipated worst case scenario while mobile is the critical determinant. If the resource is easily and reliably available at all visited locations, then it can be treated as a pervasive resource. If there is reasonable doubt about its availability or attributes at even one visited location, then it has to be treated as portable. Using this resource-centric viewpoint, this article highlights the unique role of the cloud in mobile computing, as well as its limitations. It discusses how some legacy systems can be rearchitected using modern technology, and how emerging real-time mobile AI systems will have to straddle the “carry” versus“find” divide.

title: The O-RAN Evolution Path Toward 6G AI-Native RAN
authors: Qingtian Wang, Yue Wang, Longyu Zhou, Jun Hyuk Song, Zexu Li, Jingyi Wang
abstract: Radio Access Network (RAN) evolves from a dedicated RAN to a virtual RAN and Open RAN (O-RAN). The evolution is required with an Artificial Intelligence (AI) service for future 6G applications. The AI is also embedded into the network to represent as AI-native RAN which is one of the focuses of the next-generation RAN. In particular, O-RAN is in advance of intelligent RAN, it provides a cloud-based and open architecture for wireless networks, separates traditional RAN components, designs open interfaces, and introduces the RAN Intelligent Controller (RIC) to enhance wireless networks’ scalability, flexibility, and intelligence. However, integrating AI into O-RAN presents challenges, including alignment with other standards and performance limitations and integration cost in diverse vendors. This article discusses the role of O-RAN in 6G networks. We first analyze the requirements of emerging applications and introduce the AI-native O-RAN for 6G. We then address the question, “How open is open?” and outline the RIC evaluation path for 6G. In addition, we explore the potential alignment between 3GPP and O-RAN. Finally, we identify several promising O-RAN research directions to inspire and guide future studies.

title: Non-linear Chirp Interference Mitigation in Snapshot GNSS Receivers Using Piecewise Least Squares Estimation with Fractional Fourier Transform
authors: Xavier Álvarez-Molina, Gonzalo Seco-Granados, José A. López-Salcedo
publish: IEEE Journal of Indoor and Seamless Positioning and Navigation
abstract: Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS) face increasing vulnerability to radio frequency interference, with chirp signals representing a particularly challenging threat due to their time-varying frequency characteristics that disrupt receivers across wide bandwidths. This study introduces a novel interference mitigation technique that partitions the interferer into segments, leveraging the Fractional Fourier Transform (FrFT) to enhance suppression. The proposed algorithm employs a piecewise least squares estimation (LSE) method in combination with fractional domain processing, specifically aimed at manage non-linear wideband chirp interference impacting snapshot GNSS receivers. A Generalized Cross Validation (GCV) metric is used to balance algorithmic complexity with fitting accuracy in each segment. Mitigation occurs in the fractional domain, allowing interference energy to be concentrated at fractional coordinates matching the chirp characteristics, thereby reducing the number of affected GNSS samples compare to traditional pulse blanking. The method demonstrates particular robustness against linear frequency modulated (LFM) chirps, and for quadratic frequency modulated (QFM) signals, it further divides the interference into approximately LFM segments for targeted FrFT-based suppression. Experimental results show that this approach preserves signal quality more effectively than conventional mitigation strategies.

title: Spatiotemporal Graph Latent Diffusion Models for Human Action Synthesis
authors: Bruno Degardin, Vasco Lopes, João Neves, Hugo Proença
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biometrics, Behavior, and Identity Science
abstract: Generating natural human actions and behaviours remains a complex task, encompassing first, the refinement of generated forms; second, the enrichment of their diversity; and third, achieving temporal flexibility. This complexity is especially evident in the task of synthesising lifelike body movements tailored to specific actions, often referred to as action conditioning. This paper introduces GLDM, a new architecture that builds on Latent Diffusion Models (DMs) and Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) to synthesise human motions effectively. By decomposing the process of motion formation into a sequence of denoising graph autoencoders (GAEs) within a graph latent space, it is possible to generate unconstrained body movements while improving sample quality, diversity, conditioning and temporal flexibility. The experiments carried out in three well-known datasets show that GLDM notably outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in terms of distribution quality metrics while having the ability to generate over 120 distinct actions and behaviours, including text prompt conditioning. Furthermore, it is demonstrated that the improvements attained in the generation of synthetic behaviours allow the increase of the accuracy of action recognition methods when trained on these data.

title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting with Large Language Models for Inclusive Learning: Advancing Problem-Solving in Engineering Education and Research
authors: Taiwo Feyijimi, Sarah Jane Bork
abstract: This conceptual paper presents an innovative framework for integrating Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting within engineering education to enhance the understanding, assessment, and facilitation of complex problem-solving skills. Traditional assessment methods often focus on final answers, overlooking the critical reasoning steps that reveal cognitive barriers and provide valuable insights for tailored pedagogical interventions. By utilizing the advanced reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs), CoT prompting guides students through structured and iterative problem- solving processes, cultivating metacognitive awareness and fostering a deeper engagement with engineering concepts. The proposed CoT prompting with LLM (CoT- LLM) framework not only assesses the outcomes of problem-solving but also emphasizes the reasoning pathways students undertake, offering a dual lens for evaluation. This approach bridges cognitive science, engineering pedagogy, and AI methodologies, enabling educators to identify reasoning gaps and develop equity-focused teaching strategies. Furthermore, the CoT-LLM framework mitigates the risks of overreliance on AI tools by reinforcing human-centered problem-solving skills, ensuring students remain at the core of the learning process. This paper highlights the transformative potential of CoT prompting to democratize STEM education, particularly in resource-constrained settings where traditional teaching methods may be limited. By emphasizing transparent and inclusive learning processes, the CoT-LLM framework offers a pathway to address critical educational inequities. Ultimately, this work advocates for a paradigm shift in STEM education—one that integrates advanced AI-driven techniques to foster a more inclusive, equitable, and effective educational landscape, equipping students with the cognitive and collaborative tools necessary for real-world engineering challenges.

title: Hybrid Reconfigurable Intelligent Surface-Based Reflection Modulation for MISO Communications
authors: Junzhou Xiong, Guoquan Li, Fei Liu, Jinzhao Lin, Yu Pang
abstract: Reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RISs) can not only enhance communication performance but also convey additional information through tunable reflection coefficients. However, due to the multiplicative fading effect, the potential of conventional passive RISs is limited. In this paper, an active-passive hybrid RIS-based reflection modulation (HRIS-RM) scheme is proposed for multiple-input single-output (MISO) communications. In the proposed HRIS-RM scheme, all reflecting elements (REs) on the RIS are partitioned into groups, each of which operates either in an active or a passive state. The hybrid RIS assists the multi-antenna transmitter (Tx) in beamforming while conveying additional information through the number of active RE groups. Signal models and detection methods are developed for both continuous and discrete RIS phase-shift conditions. Moreover, upper bounds on the theoretical bit error probabilities (BEPs) for both Tx information and RIS information are derived under these two conditions. The HRIS-RM scheme is further extended to the case of imperfect channel state information, and its theoretical BEP performance is analyzed. Simulation results validate the theoretical analysis and demonstrate that the proposed HRIS-RM scheme consistently outperforms existing benchmark schemes in terms of error performance under both continuous and discrete RIS phase-shift conditions.

title: Experimental Study of Underwater Acoustical Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces With Synthetic Reflection
authors: Yu Luo, Lina Pu, Aijun Song
abstract: This article presents an underwater acoustical reconfigurable intelligent surface (UA-RIS) designed for long-range, high-speed, and environmentally friendly communication in oceanic environments. The proposed UA-RIS comprises multiple pairs of acoustical reflectors that utilize a synthetic reflection scheme to flexibly control the amplitude and phase of reflected waves. This capability enables precise beam steering to enhance or attenuate sound levels in specific directions. A prototype UA-RIS with 4 × 6 acoustical reflection units is constructed and tested in both tank and lake environments to evaluate performance. Experimental results using a continuous wave as the source signal demonstrate that the prototype is capable of effectively pointing reflected waves to targeted directions while minimizing sidelobes through synthetic reflection. Field tests reveal that deploying the UA-RIS on the sender side considerably extends communication ranges by 28% in deep water and 46% in shallow waters. Furthermore, with a fixed communication distance, positioning the UA-RIS at the transmitter side substantially boosts the receiving signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), with an average increase of 2.13 dB and peaks up to 2.92 dB. When positioned on the receiver side, the UA-RIS can expand the communication range in shallow and deep water environments by 40.6% and 66%, respectively. Moreover, placing the UA-RIS close to the receiver enhances SNR by an average of 2.56 dB, reaching up to 4.2 dB under certain circumstances.

title: Faint Bottom Echo Detection for Airborne LiDAR Bathymetry Based on a Constrained Waveform Stacking Model
authors: Xiaolin Yu, Dianpeng Su, Fanlin Yang, Wenze Yang, Zhenhui Wang, Chunxiao Wang, Anxiu Yang, Chao Qi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Airborne LiDAR bathymetry is particularly suited for acquiring three-dimensional spatial information in shallow water zones. However, its signal undergoes rapid attenuate in the water column, resulting in faint bottom echoes that are challenging to detect and directly limit the maximum bathymetric performance. To achieve accurate extraction of these echoes, a faint bottom echo detection method based on a constrained waveform stacking model is proposed. The model employs distances between water-surface points and their refraction vectors as dual constraints to filter spatially adjacent waveforms. The filtered waveforms are then utilized to construct a pseudo waveform with high signal-to-noise ratio by waveform stacking. On this basis, the accurate faint bottom echo moment is subsequently determined based on a normalized peak significance score. The proposed method is validated using two Mapper-20KU datasets acquired from a coastal zone and an inland reservoir in Qingdao, China. The results demonstrate that the method increases the maximum bathymetric depth by approximately 50% in both regions and significantly improves the point density. Compared with existing topographic data, the root mean square errors (RMSEs) of the derived bathymetric points are 0.22 m and 0.09 m, respectively. Therefore, the proposed method can significantly enhance the maximum bathymetric depth while ensuring the bathymetric accuracy.

title: Unveiling the Multifaceted Landscape of Biohacking: Insights from a Machine Learning-Driven Systematic Review_supp1-3679976.docx
authors: Vito Santamato

title: Dynamical System Approach for Optimal Control Problems with Equilibrium Constraints Using Gap-Constraint-Based Reformulation
authors: Kangyu Lin, Toshiyuki Ohtsuka
abstract: This study focuses on using direct methods (first-discretize-then-optimize) to solve optimal control problems for a class of nonsmooth dynamical systems governed by differential variational inequalities (DVI), called optimal control problems with equilibrium constraints (OCPECs). In the discretization step, we propose a class of novel approaches to smooth the DVI. The generated smoothing approximations of the DVI, referred to as gap-constraint-based reformulations, have computational advantages owing to their concise and semismoothly differentiable constraint system. In the optimization step, we propose an efficient dynamical system approach to solve the discretized OCPEC, where a sequence of its smoothing approximations is solved approximately. This system approach involves a semismooth Newton flow, thereby achieving fast local exponential convergence. We confirm the effectiveness of our method using a numerical example.

title: LAGTrust: Language-Augmented Graph Representation Learning for Trust Evaluation in Cloud-Network Convergence
authors: Fan Pan, Pengshuai Cui, Jiaqi Zhang, Jinchuan Pei, Xuanyan Song, Yuxiang Hu, Keping Long
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: Cloud-network convergence represents a pivotal architectural evolution in modern communication networks, enabling unified orchestration of computing, storage, and network resources. Within such environment, trust evaluation plays an essential role in ensuring secure resource scheduling and reliable service deployment. While graph representation learning (GRL) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for trust evaluation by capturing complex relational patterns among entities, existing approaches still face three fundamental challenges: resource heterogeneity, data sparsity, and insufficient interpretability. To address these challenges, we propose LAGTrust, a Language-Augmented Graph representation learning framework for trust evaluation in cloud-network convergence. LAGTrust integrates large language models (LLMs) with graph neural networks through three synergistic components: (i) a Heterogeneous Feature Representation module that leverages LLMs to project multi-dimensional resource attributes into a unified semantic space while employing spatio-temporal graph neural networks to capture structural and behavioral dynamics; (ii) an Adaptive Feature Enhancement module that performs hierarchical LLM-driven augmentation for semantic completion, relationship inference, and profile enrichment; and (iii) a Semantic Explanation Generation module that synthesizes multi-source evidence through causal reasoning to produce interpretable trust assessments. Extensive experiments on real-world and synthetic topologies demonstrate that LAGTrust consistently outperforms baseline methods across multiple evaluation metrics, particularly under cold-start scenarios, validating its superior accuracy, robustness, and interpretability for trust evaluation in cloud-network convergence environments.

title: Adversarial Bandit Learning Assisted Online Optimization for Digital Twin Placement and Update in End-Edge-Cloud Collaboration_supp1-3677210.pdf
authors: changyan yi
abstract: Digital twin (DT) is envisioned not only to perform the high-fidelity virtual representation of its corresponding physical entity (PE), but also to serve as an active agent delivering diverse types of sophisticated services. This paper studies an end-edge-cloud collaborative DT placement and update framework. Specifically, we consider that DTs are dynamically placed across edge servers (ESs) via migration following their paired PEs' potential mobility, while being supported by real-time data fetched from the cloud center and user ends. On top of this, we emphasize a unique feature that DTs should also be continually updated capturing the uncertain evolutions for both personalized service ability improvement and versatile service ability maintenance, where the personalization is improved by utilizing the experiential knowledge from the cloud center and their corresponding PEs, and the versatility is maintained by integrating pre-stored profiles. To maximize the long-term system-wide average weighted quality-of-service (QoS) in handling all types of PEs' service requests under the stringent system cost constraint, we formulate an online problem to jointly optimize DT migrations, service priorities towards various request types, and all related DT updating strategies. To address underlying difficulties, we propose a novel adversarial bandit learning assisted online optimization approach, called ARBOK. We first leverage the Lyapunov decomposition method to transform the long-term problem into multiple instant ones, each of which is further decoupled into two correlated subproblems. For solving one subproblem with a bilinear structure, we develop a McCormick envelopes based algorithm (MO-EL). Besides, we design an extended adversarial combinatorial multi-armed bandit algorithm (AC-BL) to tackle the other subproblem, which constructs a super arm set to resolve the issue of excessively large decision space and employs a robust scheme to handle the inherent uncertainty and non-stationarity in each super arm's loss function. We integrate both algorithms seamlessly into ARBOK and alternately execute them till the convergence. Theoretical analysis and extensive simulations show the effectiveness of the introduced dynamic DT placement and continual update framework, demonstrating that ARBOK can converge to the asymptotic optimum within a polynomial-time complexity while outperforming counterparts.

title: Guiding LLM-based Loop Invariant Synthesis via Feedback on Local Reasoning Errors
authors: Tianchi Li, Zhenyu Yan, Junhao Liu, Peng Di, Xin Zhang
publish: ACM Transactions on Programming Languages and Systems
abstract: None

title: 25-MW Compact All-Solid-State Nanosecond Square Pulse Generator With Fast Leading Edge
authors: Hao Liu, Rui Li, Wei Shang, Limin Wang, Binbin Han, Hongyang Huang, Yunpeng Wang, Kai Wang, Qifang Zhang, Zilin Tao
abstract: To address the application requirements of high-power linear transformer drivers (LTDs), a compact high-power all-solid-state nanosecond square pulse generator (NSPG) has been developed. The design incorporates a low-impedance Blumlein-type pulse forming network (Blumlein-PFN) utilizing high-energy-density multilayer ceramic capacitors (MLCCs). A high-power-density switch array is implemented, composed of MOS-controlled thyristors (MCTs), leveraging the counterflow mutual inductance effect to reduce the parasitic inductance. This design topology thereby enhances the NSPG’s power capacity and fastens the output pulse leading edge. The NSPG features a dual-board structure with an unipolar charging strategy. The dual boards independently output positive and negative pulses by adjusting the position of the switch arrays relative to the Blumlein-PFN. The pulses superimpose at the load terminal, achieving voltage doubling. This configuration effectively mitigates single-board insulation failure risks and simplifies the charging power supply architecture as well. Furthermore, the diode protection circuits are proposed to enhance operational robustness even under the occasional mismatched-load conditions. The simulations and test results demonstrate that the NSPG’s impedance is around <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$8.7~\Omega $</tex-math> </inline-formula>. The peak voltage and current of the output pulse are 14.9 kV and 1.8 kA, respectively, when operating at a charging voltage of 8 kV with a nearly matched load of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$8.3~\Omega $</tex-math> </inline-formula>.
